<sub>FIG. 9. (a-c) Onto the lower Floquet band projected EGP ϕπ EGP(t) = arg (Zπ(t)), obtained from Cπ NESS(t), plotted over t within a period t ∈[0, T], for a chain length of L = 6, OBC and the same system parameters as in Fig. 6, for ω = 0.40 (a), ω = 0.80 (b) and ω = 1.2 (c). The plots show that ϕπ EGP(t) itself is not quantised but strongly indicate that accumulated differential changes in the EGP ∆ϕπ EGP within a period T are. (d-f) winding of U π(t) ≡Zπ(t) as t traverses the interval t ∈[0, T] for the same parameters as in (a-c) and ω = 0.40 (d), ω = 0.80 (e) and ω = 1.2 (f). In (d) and (e) Zπ(t) encircles the origin (red dots) twice (nontrivial) in the counterclockwise direction and in...</sub>

</details>

**Main problem.**

Investigating whether the topological classification of isolated Floquet systems persists in driven-dissipative, mixed-state settings at finite temperatures.

**Main result.**

The study establishes that a Z x Z topological classification exists for the driven-dissipative SSH chain, characterized by a pair of invariants associated with the 0 and pi quasienergy gaps.

**Method.**

The authors use Floquet-Born-Markov theory to model system-bath coupling and the Ensemble Geometric Phase (EGP) to generalize topological invariants to mixed states.

**Summary.**

This paper explores how topology survives in quantum systems that are both periodically driven and subject to dissipation. By studying a driven SSH chain, the authors show that the complex topological structure (Z x Z) found in isolated systems remains intact even when the system is coupled to thermal baths. They introduce the Ensemble Geometric Phase as a way to track these topological features in mixed states. The results demonstrate that topological edge modes can be protected in both the 0 and pi quasienergy gaps of the steady state.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.**

A one-dimensional, periodically driven Su-Schrieffer-Heeger (SSH) chain of Majorana fermions coupled to thermal reservoirs.

**Key observables.**

Ensemble Geometric Phase (EGP), purity spectrum, quasienergy spectrum, and Liouvillian rapidity spectrum.

**Important parameters / regimes.**

Driving frequency (omega), driving strength (V), hopping amplitudes (t, t'), and reservoir temperature.

**Assumptions / limitations.**

The system is treated within the Born-Markov approximation (weak coupling) and the steady state is assumed to be a Gaussian quadratic state.

**Figures summary.**

Figures illustrate the SSH model geometry, the formation of Floquet replicas and edge states in the quasienergy spectrum, the purity spectrum gaps, and the winding of the EGP in the complex plane.

**Paper structure.**

The paper introduces the driven-dissipative SSH model, develops the Floquet-Born-Markov framework for Gaussian states, defines the Ensemble Geometric Phase, and then applies these tools to identify topological invariants and phase transitions.

**Why it may be interesting.**

It provides a robust theoretical framework for extending Floquet topology from isolated, zero-temperature systems to realistic, dissipative, and finite-temperature non-equilibrium steady states.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We investigate the non-equilibrium topology of a periodically driven, dissipative Su-Schrieffer-Heeger chain using the ensemble geometric phase (EGP) $φ_{\mathrm{EGP}}$-a generalisation of the Zak phase to open quantum systems. In contrast to earlier work, we use Floquet-Born-Markov theory to describe the coupling to thermal reservoirs microscopically. We show that the steady state can be characterised by a Hermitian purity spectrum, providing a direct analogue of band topology for mixed states. The periodic drive induces nontrivial winding and a quasienergy spectrum with distinct $0$ and $π$ band gaps, with protected edge modes in each gap. We identify a pair of topological invariants $(φ^{0}_{\mathrm{EGP}}, Δφ^π_{\mathrm{EGP}})$, revealing a structure consistent with a $\mathbb{Z}\times\mathbb{Z}$ classification known from isolated Floquet SSH systems, and show how it extends to a dissipative, finite-temperature setting in regimes where the steady-state structure remains well defined. Our results demonstrate when and how known Floquet topology survives in a driven-dissipative Gaussian steady state and establish Floquet topology as a robust concept beyond isolated zero-temperature systems. The underlying formalism provides a general framework for quadratic fermionic systems with linear bath couplings.

</details>

**Main problem.**

The need for high-speed, robust, and truly unpredictable quantum random number generation (QRNG) using locally available hardware rather than cloud-based systems.

**Main result.**

Demonstrated a time-of-arrival QRNG using NV centers in nanodiamonds that achieves generation rates up to 4.77 Mbits/s, passing NIST and ENT statistical tests without post-processing.

**Method.**

A time-of-arrival scheme measuring the arrival times of photons emitted by NV centers, combined with theoretical min-entropy estimation and statistical validation using NIST and ENT suites.

**Summary.**

This paper demonstrates a high-speed quantum random number generator using the arrival times of photons from nitrogen-vacancy centers in nanodiamonds. By using clusters of up to 50 NV centers, the researchers achieved generation rates significantly higher than previous NV-based methods. The generated bits passed rigorous statistical tests without needing any post-processing or randomness extraction. The work suggests a path toward highly compact, on-chip quantum cryptographic components.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.**

Nitrogen-vacancy (NV) centers in fluorescent nanodiamonds, modeled as a three-level system (ground, excited, and metastable shelving states) with potential background contamination.

**Key observables.**

Photon detection rate, second-order temporal correlation function g2(tau), min-entropy, and fluorescence spectra.

**Important parameters / regimes.**

Generation rates (0.173 to 4.77 Mbits/s), photon flux (lambda), and the number of NV centers (N) ranging from 1 to ~50.

**Assumptions / limitations.**

Assumes at most one photon arrives during the time interval T, and that the photon flux is time-independent.

**Figures summary.**

Fig 1: Confocal microscopy setup schematic; Fig 2: Fluorescence scanning maps; Fig 4: Three-level NV model; Fig 5: g2(tau) plots for different regions; Fig 7: Probability distribution and entropy plots; Fig 8: Experimental entropy and distribution results; Fig 10: Fluorescence spectra.

**Paper structure.**

The paper introduces the QRNG problem, describes the experimental confocal setup and NV center characterization, presents the time-of-arrival implementation and its performance across different NV clusters, provides a theoretical framework for entropy and uniformity, and concludes with future integration prospects.

**Why it may be interesting.**

The paper combines quantum optics (single-photon statistics) with practical quantum information tasks (QRNG), offering insights into how multi-emitter systems and source imperfections affect the entropy of quantum-derived random bits.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Quantum random number generation (QRNG) relies on the inherent unpredictability of quantum mechanical phenomena to efficiently generate high-quality random numbers that can be used in a wide range of cryptography and simulation applications. Here we report the experimental demonstration of QRNG from the arrival times of photons emitted by nitrogen vacancy (NV) centres in fluorescent nanodiamonds. The generation rates achieved range from 0.173 Mbits/s for a region with a single NV centre to 4.77 Mbits/s for a region with just under 50 NV centres, where the latter demonstrates an order of magnitude improvement compared to the highest generation rate previously achieved with NV centres. For all the regions investigated, the generated bits passed the ENT and NIST Statistical Test Suites without post-processing. The results are consistent with our theoretical analysis, where we show that the min-entropy is very close to the ideal value of one per bit for all the regions investigated. This work opens up new possibilities for robust QRNG in highly compact on-chip settings.

</details>

### [Nanoscale Sensing of Solid-State Samples with High Frequency Resolution](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.25660v1)

**Authors:** P. Alsina-Bolívar, D. B. Bucher, J. Casanova

**Type:** both · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.25660v1>

**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

**Topic relevance:** 🔥 `QC/QI experiment` **4/5** · 🔥 `spintronics-quantum-optics interface` **4/5** · `non-equilibrium dynamics` **2/5** · `quantum measurements` **2/5**

These regions exhibit distinct initial magnetizations ( ⃗M j and ⃗ M j+1) as a result of statistical polarisation. (Bottom) After a time τ each magnetization vector evolves owing to the combined action of ⃗B0(t) and Hc(t) in Eqs. (2) and (3). b) Trajectory of ⃗B0(t) in Eq. (2). This traces a cone, with aperture ϵ and central axis ˆn. The inset shows in more detail the diamond-sample block irradiated with RF and MWs. c) (Top) Signal emitted by the sample. The colored boxes correspond to the...</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<img src="2604.25660_figures/2604.25660_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>

<sub>Figure 2. Simulated spectrum for a sample with two magnetically non-equivalent protons. The solid colored lines represent the spec- tra obtained using our protocol under various misalignment errors, while gray crosses depict the typical powder spectrum (for a non- rotating magnetic field) for comparison. Dashed vertical lines in- dicate the theoretical resonances predicted by our framework. The results demonstrate the protocol’s robustness against both increas- ing misalignments and stochastic amplitude noise (0.25% fluctua- tions with a 1 ms correlation time).</sub>

</details>

**Main problem.**

Achieving high-frequency resolution in nanoscale NMR spectroscopy using NV centers is hindered by chemical shift anisotropy (CSA) and strong dipole-dipole interactions in solid-state samples.

**Main result.**

The authors propose a quantum control protocol using a rotating magnetic field and RF decoupling that successfully isolates the isotropic chemical shift and provides a robust analytical mapping for sample characterization.

**Method.**

The protocol employs a slowly rotating magnetic field to average out CSA and a frequency-switched Lee-Goldburg (fsLG) inspired RF scheme for dipolar decoupling, combined with multi-point correlation spectroscopy.

**Summary.**

This paper presents a new quantum control method for using NV centers to perform high-resolution NMR on solid-state surfaces. By synchronizing a rotating magnetic field with specific RF decoupling pulses, the researchers can suppress unwanted anisotropic interactions and dipolar couplings. This allows for the precise detection of isotropic chemical shifts, which serve as a unique fingerprint for nanoscale materials. The study demonstrates that the protocol is robust against experimental imperfections like magnetic field misalignment and amplitude noise.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.**

The system consists of an ensemble of NV centers in diamond (electron and nitrogen memory spins) acting as sensors to probe a solid-state sample containing a collection of nuclear spins (e.g., protons) in the statistical polarization regime.

**Key observables.**

Isotropic chemical shifts (delta_iso), Bloch-Siegert shifts, and the averaged electron spin state (S_z).

**Important parameters / regimes.**

Magnetic field rotation frequency (nu), RF Rabi frequency (Omega), magnetic field aperture (epsilon), and the number of spins (N).

**Assumptions / limitations.**

The rotation frequency is much smaller than the Zeeman splitting; the effective Rabi frequency is the dominant interaction; and the large N limit allows approximating nuclear spin operators with expectation values.

**Figures summary.**

Figure 1 shows the NV sensor schematic, magnetization evolution, and the rotating magnetic field trajectory. Figure 2 presents simulated NMR spectra for non-equivalent protons, demonstrating robustness against misalignment and noise.

**Paper structure.**

The paper introduces the challenge of nanoscale sensing, describes the physical model and Hamiltonian, details the proposed rotating field and RF control protocol, provides an analytical derivation of the effective Hamiltonian using Average Hamiltonian Theory, and validates the method through numerical simulations of error robustness.

**Why it may be interesting.**

The paper presents an advanced quantum control protocol for managing many-body interactions (dipolar coupling) and anisotropy, which is highly relevant to the control of spin ensembles and the development of high-precision quantum sensors.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

To meet the growing demand for nanoscale surface analysis, nitrogen-vacancy (NV) centers offer a high-sensitivity alternative by leveraging their ability to operate in immediate proximity to the sample. In this work, we propose a quantum control protocol designed to overcome the inherent challenges of solid-state environments, specifically by mitigating anisotropy and strong dipole-dipole interactions to enable the detection of isotropic chemical shifts at the nanoscale. To achieve this, our scheme synchronizes a slowly rotating magnetic field with tailored RF decoupling and MW control of the NV sensors. We provide an analytical mapping that explicitly links the measured spectrum to the control sequence features and the underlying system parameters, enabling a straightforward characterization of the sample.

</details>

### [Semi-transmitter-device-independent quantum key distribution](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.25225v1)

**Authors:** Qiang Zeng, Abhishek Mishra, Haoyang Wang, Zhiliang Yuan

**Type:** both · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.25225v1>

**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

**Topic relevance:** 🔥 `QC/QI experiment` **4/5** · 🔥 `quantum optics experiment` **4/5** · `entanglement & information structure` **2/5** · `quantum measurements` **2/5**

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2604.25225_figures/2604.25225_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>

Alice’s mea- surement device is treated as black box, and her inputs are individually selected. The entanglement source is integrated into a composable transmitter, sending one of the entangled photons to the measurement in a one-way manner with the other side being independent of Alice’s inputs.</sub>

</details>

The equivalent transmission distance is estimated through a standard telecom optical fiber with a loss of 0.2 dB/km. The red pentagram denotes the estimated secure key-rate based on our experimental data point.</sub>

</details>

The simulation adopts ηB = 25%, pA D = 1 × 10−3, and pB D = 2.6 × 10−3. The red pentagram denotes our experimen- tal data point.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 5</summary>

</details>

<img src="2604.25225_figures/2604.25225_fig6.jpg" width="500"><br>

<sub>FIG. 6. The results of full probability distribution. The ten gray bars denote the redundant correlation probabilities due to the probability conservation and no-signaling constraints.</sub>

</details>

**Main problem.**

Addressing transmitter-side vulnerabilities in quantum key distribution (QKD) by formalizing and experimentally demonstrating a semi-transmitter-device-independent (STDI) protocol.

**Main result.**

The authors successfully demonstrated the first discrete-variable 1sDI-QKD protocol, achieving a secure key rate of 1 kbps over an equivalent fiber distance of 20 km.

**Method.**

The study uses a composable transmitter approach that integrates an untrusted source with a detection module, utilizing the Devetak-Winter bound and the NPA hierarchy for security verification.

**Summary.**

This paper introduces a semi-transmitter-device-independent (STDI) protocol for quantum key distribution to mitigate vulnerabilities in the photon source. By treating the transmitter as a composable unit of an untrusted source and a black-box detector, the authors reduce the stringent requirements of full device-independence. They experimentally demonstrate this protocol using polarization entanglement, achieving a 1 kbps key rate over 20 km of fiber. This approach offers a more practical path toward secure, large-scale quantum communication networks.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.**

A polarization-entanglement-based system using a Sagnac-loop interferometer with a PPLN crystal for SPDC, featuring superconducting nanowire single-photon detectors (SNSPDs) and active wave plates.

**Key observables.**

Secure key rate, photon detection statistics (single clicks, double clicks, no-clicks), and state fidelity via tomography.

**Important parameters / regimes.**

Heralding efficiency (65%), dark count probabilities, detection efficiency (25% simulated), and a critical heralding efficiency threshold of 54.8%.

**Assumptions / limitations.**

The detectors are assumed to be indistinguishable from dark counts, and Bob's detectors are assumed to anti-commute.

**Figures summary.**

Figure 1 illustrates the proposed STDI configuration compared to standard DI; Figure 2 shows the experimental setup; Figure 5 shows state tomography results; Figure 6 compares experimental probability distributions with theoretical predictions.

**Paper structure.**

The paper introduces the problem of transmitter-side dependence, proposes the STDI protocol, details the experimental setup and detection modeling, presents security analysis via numerical optimization, and concludes with experimental validation and performance metrics.

**Why it may be interesting.**

This work is highly relevant to quantum optics and quantum networks as it provides a practical bridge between high-security device-independent protocols and the high-loss tolerance required for real-world fiber-based quantum communication.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Transmitter-device-dependence is a longstanding but often implicit problem in quantum key distribution (QKD), as compared to measurement-device-dependence. One-sided device-independent (1sDI) scenario relaxes the security conditions of DI framework and offers an intuitive solution to transmitter-sided dependence. Here we show that, by constructing a composable transmitter in a 1sDI configuration, the transmitter-device-dependence can be largely eliminated. Our scheme integrates the entanglement source into the transmitter as an individual part, and the detection module is another individual part treated as black box. In a proof-of-principle experiment we obtain a secure key-rate of 1~kbps at an equivalent transmission fiber distance of 20~km, demonstrating the first discrete-variable 1sDI-QKD. By implementing semi-transmitter-device-independent security while maintaining strong loss tolerance, our approach bridges security and practicality for real-world 1sDI-QKD deployments.

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

<a id="paper-2604.25229"></a>

### [Hardware Realization of a Hamiltonian Simulation Algorithm for Time-Domain Maxwells Equations](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.25229v1)

**Authors:** Gautam Sharma, Apurva Tiwari, Niladri Gomes, Jezer Jojo, J. Eric Bracken, Jay Pathak

**Type:** both · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.25229v1>

**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

**Topic relevance:** 🔥 `QC/QI experiment` **4/5** · `non-equilibrium dynamics` **2/5** · `quantum measurements` **2/5** · `quantum optics experiment` **2/5**

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2604.25229_figures/2604.25229_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>

From top to bottom, the rows show the evolution of the field components Ez, Hx, and Hy, respectively. Columns correspond to snapshots at times T = 0, 8, 16, and 24.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<img src="2604.25229_figures/2604.25229_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>

From top to bottom, the rows show the evolution of the field components Ez, Hx, and Hy, respectively. Columns correspond to snapshots at times T = 0, 5, 10, and 15.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 3</summary>

<img src="2604.25229_figures/2604.25229_fig3.jpg" width="500"><br>

<sub>Fig. 3: Three-dimensional evolution of the magnetic-field component Hx shown as an isosurface on a 16 × 16 × 16 grid with PMC boundary conditions. The fields are excited by an initially localized point source in the Ez component at the center of the domain, (x0, y0, z0) = (8, 8, 8).</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 4</summary>

<img src="2604.25229_figures/2604.25229_fig4.jpg" width="500"><br>

<sub>Fig. 4: Orthogonal two-dimensional cross sections of the Hx field taken through the center of the domain in the xy, xz, and yz planes at times T = 5, 10.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 5</summary>

<img src="2604.25229_figures/2604.25229_fig5.jpg" width="500"><br>

<sub>Fig. 5: Comparison of field values measured at the center of the 16×16 computational grid, (8, 8), where the initial electric-field excitation is applied. Time evolution of the field components Ez, Hx, and Hy is shown for classical simulation, noise-free quantum simulation, and quantum hardware execution. The noise-free quantum results closely follow the analytical solution, while the hardware results qualitatively reproduce the correct temporal behavior despite the presence of device noise.</sub>

</details>

**Main problem.**

Developing a practical quantum-hardware implementation for simulating the time-domain Maxwell equations, specifically overcoming the challenges of non-unitary dynamics and the extraction of signed vector-field values.

**Main result.**

The authors demonstrate the first quantum-hardware implementation of a Hamiltonian simulation algorithm for signed vector-field solutions, showing that the IonQ QPU can qualitatively reproduce electromagnetic field evolution.

**Method.**

The approach uses Schrödingerisation to transform non-unitary dynamics into a unitary form, Bell-basis decomposition for efficient circuit construction, and ADAPT-AQC for circuit compression, alongside a relative-phase-based sign reconstruction technique.

**Summary.**

This paper presents the first experimental demonstration of a quantum algorithm capable of computing and measuring time-evolving electromagnetic vector fields on real quantum hardware. By using Schrödingerisation and Bell-basis decomposition, the authors map the non-unitary Maxwell equations onto a unitary quantum circuit. They successfully implemented this on an IonQ trapped-ion processor, using circuit compression techniques to make the depth manageable for NISQ-era devices. The results show that the hardware can accurately capture the temporal evolution and reflections of electromagnetic waves.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.**

The system involves electromagnetic field propagation in a homogeneous, lossless, and isotropic medium, discretized on a 3D Yee finite-difference time-domain grid and implemented on an IonQ trapped-ion quantum processor.

**Key observables.**

Magnitudes and physical directions (signs) of the electric (E) and magnetic (H) field components.

**Important parameters / regimes.**

Grid dimensions (up to 16x16x16), time steps (dt = 0.1, 0.01), and qubit requirements (n = log2 N + 3).

**Assumptions / limitations.**

The medium is assumed to be homogeneous, lossless, and isotropic; the simulation is source-free; and the boundary conditions are limited to PEC and PMC types.

**Figures summary.**

Figures show snapshots of E and H field evolution in 2D and 3D grids, including reflections from PEC scatterers, and a comparison between classical, noise-free, and hardware-based field values.

**Paper structure.**

The paper progresses from the continuous Maxwell equations to a discrete matrix-based ODE system, introduces the Schrödingerisation and Bell-basis framework, details the hardware implementation and circuit compression, and concludes with numerical validation on IonQ hardware.

**Why it may be interesting.**

This work is highly relevant for quantum optics and many-body dynamics as it provides a foundational framework for using quantum hardware to solve complex classical electromagnetic problems, potentially bridging the gap between quantum computing and computational electromagnetics.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We present the first quantum-hardware implementation of a Hamiltonian simulation algorithm that produces signed vector-field solutions to the time-domain Maxwells equations using a Schrodingerisation-based approach.The electromagnetic fields are discretized using finite-difference operators, and the resulting non-unitary matrices are mapped to Bell-basis Trotter blocks, enabling efficient circuit construction. We introduce a measurement procedure that retrieves not only field amplitudes, but also physical directions of the electric and magnetic field values at select spatial points. Implementing this logic on quantum hardware relies on relative-phase-based sign reconstruction. Numerical results obtained using IonQ QPU, show good agreement with analytical solutions of benchmark problems in two dimensions and on simulators; in three dimensions. We further extend our approach to compute fields scattered from simple bodies, by enforcing appropriate boundary conditions. Our work lays the foundational steps towards realizing quantum-hardware solutions for computational electromagnetics.

</details>

### [Near-identical photons from distant quantum dot-cavity devices](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.25615v1)

**Authors:** Thibaut Pollet, Victor Guilloux, Duc-Duy Tran, Anton Pishchagin, Stephen Wein, Joseph A. Sulpizio, William Hease, Petr Stepanov, Petr Steindl, Nico Margaria, Samuel Mister, Martina Morassi, Aristide Lemaître, Thi Huong Au, Sébastien Boissier, Pascale Senellart

**Main problem.**

Achieving high-visibility quantum interference (indistinguishability) between single photons emitted from spatially distant and independent semiconductor quantum dot-cavity sources.

**Main result.**

Demonstrated a record mutual indistinguishability of 88 +/- 1% between two distant sources, reaching the upper bound set by the intrinsic performance of the individual emitters.

**Method.**

The researchers used a combination of strain-tuning (via piezo-driven fiber) and electrical tuning (via PIN diode Stark shift) to align wavelengths, followed by Hong-Ou-Mandel interference measurements.

**Summary.**

This paper reports on the successful creation of near-identical photons from two separate, distant quantum dot-cavity devices. By using a dual-tuning approach involving both strain and electric fields, the researchers were able to overcome wavelength mismatches and spectral noise. They achieved a high mutual indistinguishability of 88%, which is nearly the maximum possible value allowed by the individual sources. This achievement is a significant milestone for scaling up photonic quantum technologies and building large-scale quantum networks.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.**

The platform consists of two independent InGaAs quantum dot-cavity micropillars embedded in a PIN diode structure, cooled to 5 K, using longitudinal acoustic (LA) phonon-assisted excitation.

**Key observables.**

Two-photon indistinguishability (M12), Hong-Ou-Mandel visibility (V_HOM), second-order correlation function (g2(0)), and spectral noise/fluctuations.

**Important parameters / regimes.**

Indistinguishability of 88%, cavity Q factor of 8000, charge noise at 4.8% of natural linewidth, and photon collection efficiency between 15.6% and 27.5%.

**Assumptions / limitations.**

The calculation of the theoretical upper bound assumes perfect temporal overlap (s12 = 1) between the two photons.

**Figures summary.**

Fig 1: SEM images and fabrication details showing wavelength/cavity distribution and low charge noise. Fig 2: Principles of phonon-assisted excitation and lifetime/Purcell effect measurements. Fig 3: Extraction of mutual indistinguishability and comparison to theoretical bounds. Extended Data: Fabrication process, g2(0) measurements, and optical setup schematics.

**Paper structure.**

The paper introduces the challenge of remote interference, describes the fabrication and tuning architecture, presents the characterization of individual source stability, demonstrates the successful interference of distant sources, and concludes with the scalability and future potential of the approach.

**Why it may be interesting.**

This work is highly relevant to quantum optics and quantum networks as it demonstrates a scalable method to bridge the gap between single-source performance and remote-source interference, a prerequisite for photon-mediated entanglement in quantum computing.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Scalable optical quantum technologies require interference between large numbers of indistinguishable single-photons emitted by independent sources. Semiconductor quantum dots are known to be excellent on-demand sources of single-photons. They show record efficiency when inserted into optical cavities to control their spontaneous emission and generate trains of near identical photons over microsecond timescales. However, generating perfectly identical photons from distant cavity-based sources has remained a long-standing challenge. It requires precise matching of the emission wavelengths and emission dynamics, while simultaneously minimizing spectral noise across all time scales for distant emitters in uncorrelated environments. Here, we report on the nanofabrication of a large number of quantum dot-cavity sources with ultra-low spectral noise and wavelength dispersion. The high source efficiency and the use of two tuning mechanisms enable precise optimization of the spectral overlap between distant sources. With this approach, we demonstrate a two-photon indistinguishability of $88\pm1$ % between photons emitted from two distant sources. Remarkably, this value reaches the upper bound set by the intrinsic indistinguishability of photons emitted successively by each source. These results represent a key milestone for scaling photon-based quantum technologies.

</details>

**Main problem.**

The difficulty of experimentally determining the Fermi energy in diamond due to the highly non-linear relationship between Fermi energy and donor-acceptor concentrations.

**Main result.**

The development of a non-destructive method to determine the Fermi energy by analyzing the relative populations of NV and SiV charge states via photoluminescence spectroscopy.

**Method.**

The method combines photoluminescence (PL) spectral decomposition with Density Functional Theory (DFT) models to relate charge state populations to the Fermi energy.

**Summary.**

This paper presents a new experimental method to precisely determine the Fermi energy in diamond using photoluminescence spectroscopy. By measuring the ratio of different charge states of NV and SiV centers and applying DFT-based models, the researchers can map the Fermi energy across different diamond types. The study also demonstrates how this energy level affects spin coherence times and charge state stability. This technique is highly advantageous because it offers high spatial and temporal resolution and can function in extreme environments.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.**

The physical system consists of various diamond crystals (CVD, HPHT, and polycrystalline) containing nitrogen-vacancy (NV) and silicon-vacancy (SiV) defect centers.

**Key observables.**

Relative populations of NV- and NV0 centers, spin coherence time (T2), and PL intensity.

**Important parameters / regimes.**

Fermi energy (Ef), nitrogen donor concentration (P1 centers), and laser power dependence.

**Assumptions / limitations.**

The method relies on the accuracy of DFT-derived formation energies and assumes the ability to extrapolate populations to zero laser power to avoid laser-induced charge state conversion.

**Figures summary.**

Figure 1 shows energy diagrams and DFT-calculated formation energies; Figure 2 displays PL spectra and the results of spectral decomposition analysis; Figure 3 shows the dependence of parameters on specific variables.

**Paper structure.**

The paper introduces the problem of Fermi energy determination, presents the proposed PL-based method using DFT integration, demonstrates the method on various nitrogen-doped and thermal-grade diamond samples, investigates the correlation between Fermi energy and spin coherence (T2), extends the method to SiV centers, and concludes with the method's advantages and potential applications.

**Why it may be interesting.**

This is relevant for quantum information because controlling the charge state stability and spin coherence of NV centers is critical for the development of robust solid-state qubits and quantum sensors.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Electronic band structures and the Fermi energy provide essential information for understanding the electronic properties of solids. In semiconductors, the Fermi energy level is determined by the donor and acceptor concentrations. For diamond, the relationship between the Fermi energy level and the donor-acceptor concentrations is highly nonlinear; therefore, experimental determination of the Fermi energy level is important. Here, we report a method to determine the Fermi energy of diamond based on photoluminescence (PL) measurement. The density-functional-theory (DFT) study by Deák et al.~\cite{deak2014formation} showed the relationship between the Fermi energy and the formation energies of nitrogen-vacancy centers in the negatively charged (NV-) and neutrally charged (NV0) charge states. In the present method, we measure the relative populations of the NV- and NV0 centers from PL spectral analysis and, using these populations and the DFT result, determine the Fermi energy of the diamond samples. Moreover, we show the application of the method to study the spin coherence and the stability against the charge state conversion of the NV centers on several diamond samples. We also extend the method for the Fermi energy determination using the silicon-vacancy (SiV) center in diamond. The PL-based method is advantageous for determining the Fermi energy with high spatial and fast time resolutions, even in extreme environments, and can be extended to determine various wide band gap semiconductors.

</details>

### [Nonreciprocal magnon blockade based on nonlinear effects](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.25141v1)

**Authors:** Han-Qiu Zhang, Shuang-Shuo Chu, Jian-Song Zhang, Wen-Xue Zhong, Guang-Ling Cheng

**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.25141v1>

**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

**Topic relevance:** 🔥 `spintronics-quantum-optics interface` **4/5** · `interference shaping light` **3/5** · `methods for driven-dissipative` **2/5**

Equal-time second-order correlation function log10 g(2) mm(0) versus the photon detuning ∆p and magnon detuning ∆m, the coupling strength is chosen as (c) g = 0.5κ and (d) g = 5κ. The white dotted lines represent the the interference-induced UMB condition (6χ + 2∆p + 3∆m = 0) and the white dash- dotted curves denote the condition of MB (g2 = (χ+∆p)∆m). The other parameters are κ/2π = 2.0 MHz, γ/2π = 1.0 MHz, ∆m = 10κ, and F = 0.05κ.</sub>

The blue solid lines are the second-order correlation functions for negative Kerr eﬀect coeﬃcient, log10 g(2) mm(0) are plotted as functions of the detuning ∆p/κ; χ = 1κ (blue solid line) and χ = −1κ (red dotted line) in (a); χ = 2κ (blue solid line) and χ = −2κ (red dotted line) in (b). The other parameters are κ/2π = 2.0 MHz, γ/2π = 1.0 MHz, ∆m = 10κ, F = 0.05κ, and g = 1κ.</sub>

**Main problem.**

Achieving nonreciprocal unconventional magnon blockade (NUMB) in a hybrid cavity-magnon system to prepare single-magnon resources.

**Main result.**

The study demonstrates that nonreciprocity can be achieved by adjusting the sign of the Kerr nonlinearity, providing a method to induce magnon blockade through either destructive interference or anharmonic energy splitting.

**Method.**

The authors use adiabatic elimination of the signal cavity mode, a rotating reference frame, and the probability amplitude method to find analytical solutions, validated by numerical simulations of the master equation.

**Summary.**

This paper proposes a scheme to achieve nonreciprocal magnon blockade in a hybrid system of two microwave cavities and a YIG sphere. By utilizing nonlinear effects like Kerr nonlinearity and parametric driving, the researchers show how to control the magnon blockade effect. The study identifies two distinct regimes: unconventional blockade via destructive interference and conventional blockade via energy splitting. This work offers a new method for generating single-magnon resources essential for quantum information tasks.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.**

A hybrid system consisting of two microwave cavities (pump and signal) and one Yttrium Iron Garnet (YIG) sphere, featuring nonlinear photon-photon coupling, magnetic dipole interaction, and Kerr nonlinearity.

**Key observables.**

The second-order correlation function g(2) of the magnon mode, used to identify magnon bunching, antibunching, and perfect blockade.

**Important parameters / regimes.**

Kerr nonlinearity (chi), coupling strengths (g, J, g_ms), detunings (Delta_p, Delta_m, Delta_s), and decay rates (kappa, gamma).

**Assumptions / limitations.**

The signal cavity mode is eliminated using second-order perturbation theory under the condition that its detuning is much larger than the coupling strengths.

**Figures summary.**

Figure 2 plots the log of the second-order correlation function against detuning, showing the transition between weak and strong coupling regimes and identifying specific blockade frequency conditions.

**Paper structure.**

The paper introduces the physical platform, derives an effective Hamiltonian via adiabatic elimination, presents analytical solutions for the blockade conditions, and uses numerical simulations to demonstrate nonreciprocity and the transition between different blockade regimes.

**Why it may be interesting.**

It provides a novel mechanism for controlling quantum interference and nonreciprocity in hybrid systems, which is highly relevant for developing single-photon/magnon sources and quantum information processing.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We present an alternative scheme to achieve nonreciprocal unconventional magnon blockade (NUMB) in a hybrid system formed by two microwave cavities and one yttrium iron garnet (YIG) sphere, where the pump and signal cavities interact nonlinearly with each other and the signal cavity is coupled to the YIG sphere. It is found that the nonlinear coupling occurs between the pump cavity and magnon modes due to the dispersive interactions among three bosonic modes. Meanwhile, the Kerr nonlinearity is present in the pump cavity. Based on these nonlinear effects, a nonreciprocal magnon blockade could be achieved with the help of weak parametric driving of the pump cavity. The present work provides an alternative method to prepare single magnon resource, which may be helpful for quantum information processing.

</details>

**Authors:** Dileep Singh, Riley W. Hooper, Christoph Findler, Utsab Banerjee, Dominik B. Bucher

**Type:** experiment · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.25752v1>

**Main problem.**

Traditional NMR spectroscopy is limited by weak signal strength and requires large sample volumes and high magnetic fields, making nanoscale detection difficult.

**Main result.**

The researchers demonstrated nanoscale deuterium (2H) NMR spectroscopy using NV centers in diamond, achieving a sensitivity enhancement of six to eight orders of magnitude over inductive detection at low magnetic fields.

**Method.**

The study uses NV-based correlation spectroscopy with XY8-N dynamical decoupling pulse blocks to detect statistical spin fluctuations (spin noise) from nanometer-scale volumes.

**Summary.**

This paper presents a method for performing deuterium NMR spectroscopy at the nanoscale using nitrogen-vacancy centers in diamond. By detecting spin noise rather than inductive signals, the researchers achieved a massive sensitivity boost compared to conventional NMR. The technique successfully reproduces complex quadrupolar powder patterns at much lower magnetic fields than standard equipment. This approach enables the study of molecular dynamics and phase transitions in extremely small volumes, potentially reaching the single-molecule limit.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.**

The experimental platform consists of an ensemble of nitrogen-vacancy (NV) centers in diamond implanted ~8 nm below the surface, used to sense deuterated polymers (PMMA-d8) and molecular solids (phenanthrene-d10) at low magnetic fields (~180 mT).

**Key observables.**

2H Larmor frequency, quadrupolar coupling constant (CQ), asymmetry parameter (eta), and gyromagnetic ratio (gamma).

**Important parameters / regimes.**

Low magnetic field regime (~180 mT), nanoscale detection volumes, and sensitivity enhancement of 6-8 orders of magnitude.

**Figures summary.**

Figure 1A shows the experimental geometry; 1B shows the pulse sequence; 1C shows experimental 2H spectra; 1D shows the linear Zeeman dependence of resonance frequency vs. magnetic field.

**Paper structure.**

The paper introduces the limitations of bulk NMR, describes the NV-based experimental setup and pulse sequence, presents the results for deuterium NMR in polymers and solids, validates the findings via temperature-dependent studies and bulk NMR comparison, and concludes with the potential for single-molecule spectroscopy.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectroscopy provides unparalleled access to molecular structure and dynamics but is traditionally limited by weak signal strength, requiring large sample volumes and high magnetic fields. Here, we demonstrate nanoscale deuterium (2H) NMR spectroscopy using nitrogen vacancy (NV) centers in diamond, reproducing the characteristic quadrupolar powder line shapes that are present in the conventional bulk NMR spectra. By detecting statistical spin fluctuations from nanometer scale detection volumes, our approach delivers a sensitivity enhancement of six to eight orders of magnitude over inductive detection while operating at magnetic fields two orders of magnitude lower than those used in conventional NMR. Temperature dependent measurements of a deuterated polymer and molecular solid reveal distinct motional averaging and phase transitions with nanoscale sensitivity. Powder-like NV detected 2H NMR establishes a powerful tool for probing molecular dynamics on the nanoscale and, in the ultimate limit, at the single molecule level - capabilities beyond those of most existing spectroscopic techniques.

</details>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 4</summary>

<img src="2604.25193_figures/2604.25193_fig4.jpg" width="500"><br>

<sub>Figure 1: Reachable count-tuple DAG for a Ramsey-like DP at K = 3 epochs, J = 2 delays, n = 1 shot per epoch. Nodes: reachable count-tuples (N1, M1; N2, M2), one column per epoch; colour = posterior mean ˆx (plasma scale); size ∝posterior precision 1/σ; red ring marks nodes reached by more than one parent path (the memoization collapse). Edges: colour by action (τ1 / τ2), style by outcome (y = 0 dashed / y = 1 solid). The compression-ratio box in the lower right compares the naive playout-tree size to the count-tuple DAG size. Right column: four representative posteriors — the prior at the root and three distinct terminal-belief shapes at k = 3.</sub>

</details>

**Main problem.**

The lack of end-to-end co-design between physical hardware geometry and adaptive measurement policies, which leads to information loss at the analog-to-digital boundary.

**Main result.**

The development of a 'joint-DP' framework that achieves massive performance gains in sensing, including a 123x reduction in MSE for photonic metasensors and an 11.3x reduction for superconducting qubit flux sensors.

**Method.**

A joint dynamic programming (joint-DP) approach using differentiable dynamic programming and the envelope theorem to compute exact, unbiased gradients of the hardware geometry without smoothing the Bellman maximum.

**Summary.**

This paper introduces a new optimization framework called 'joint-DP' for the simultaneous end-to-end design of physical hardware and adaptive measurement policies. By using the envelope theorem to differentiate through the Bellman equation, the authors avoid the biases introduced by traditional smoothing methods. The framework demonstrates significant improvements in sensing precision across radar, superconducting qubits, and photonic metasurfaces. This approach ensures that the hardware is specifically optimized to exploit the information gained during adaptive measurement steps.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.**

A general framework for sensing modeled as Partially Observable Markov Decision Processes (POMDPs), applied to three specific systems: a radar beam-search, a superconducting-qubit flux sensor (Ramsey spectroscopy), and a high-dimensional photonic metasensor.

**Key observables.**

Mean-Squared Error (MSE), Mutual Information (MI), Expected Fisher log-determinant, and Expected Value of Perfect Information (EVPI).

**Important parameters / regimes.**

Hardware geometry (c), adaptive measurement policy (pi), belief state (b), and the number of sensing epochs (K).

**Assumptions / limitations.**

The hardware is designed once and remains fixed for the lifetime of the device; the inner policy is Bellman-optimal; and the computational cost of the Bellman recursion must fit within memory.

**Paper structure.**

The paper introduces the problem of fragmented optimization, proposes the joint-DP framework, details the mathematical derivation of the 'sharp-max' gradient via the envelope theorem, presents a hierarchy of relaxation methods for scalability, and validates the method through three diverse case studies (radar, SQUID, and photonics).

**Why it may be interesting.**

This is highly relevant for quantum optics and open quantum systems because it provides a principled way to optimize the design of quantum sensors and measurement protocols (like Ramsey spectroscopy) by simultaneously optimizing the physical device and the adaptive control sequence.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Inverse design has made vast physical parameter spaces a substrate for emergent behavior. In sensing, the stakes of this principle are sharpest at the analog-to-digital boundary, where any information the hardware fails to capture is information no downstream algorithm can recover; hardware optimization alone is therefore not enough, and the geometry must be co-designed with a rule for what to measure next. We formulate this co-design as \emph{joint dynamic programming} (joint-DP): a single optimization over the continuous hardware geometry and a Bellman-optimal adaptive measurement policy. The outer hardware gradient is computed by differentiable dynamic programming with a sharp Bellman maximum, which the envelope theorem makes exact and bias-free, and a relaxation hierarchy carries the common framework from small discrete POMDPs to $10^5$-pixel photonic topologies. Across three case studies, joint-DP beats the natural baseline of its community by a large factor: on a radar beam-search POMDP, classical information-bound-guided geometry selection loses $2.8\times$ in attainable adaptive value; on a superconducting-qubit flux sensor, joint-DP reduces deployed mean-squared error by $11.3\times$ over the joint Bayesian Cramér--Rao baseline, with both geometries numerically co-optimized under their respective objectives; and on a $90{,}000$-pixel photonic metasensor, joint-DP via the Bayesian Fisher-information-matrix surrogate reduces deployed mean-squared error by $123\times$ relative to a randomized baseline. For any sensor whose hardware is designed once but whose policy runs for the device's lifetime, joint optimization of hardware and policy is the minimum principled procedure.

</details>

**Main problem.**

Estimating ground-state energies of Ising models using short-time evolution on near-term quantum hardware while managing the impact of hardware errors and decoherence.

**Main result.**

The CVQE algorithm effectively finds ground-state energies for up to 63 qubits, demonstrating that the number of significant basis states scales subexponentially, though errors increase with qubit number and coupling strength.

**Method.**

The Cascaded Variational Quantum Eigensolver (CVQE) uses a Guided-Sampling Ansatz (GSA) where short-time adiabatic state preparation generates a subspace for classical eigenvalue solving.

**Summary.**

This paper presents a method to calculate the ground-state energies of Ising models using a quantum computer to simulate short-time evolution. By using the CVQE algorithm on up to 63 qubits, the authors demonstrate that the required computational subspace grows slowly with system size. They identify how hardware errors scale with coupling strength and qubit count, and propose an information ratio as a diagnostic for calculation accuracy. The work highlights the potential for near-term quantum devices to perform useful simulations of spin systems.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.**

Transverse-field Ising models (both homogeneous and random-coupling/spin-glass) on a heavy-hex lattice, implemented on the IBM ibm_torino quantum processor.

**Key observables.**

Ground-state energy (E0), average spin (sZ), information ratio (R), and number of significant basis states (N_psi).

**Important parameters / regimes.**

Coupling strength (J0 or delta J), transverse field (B), number of qubits (NQ), and the ratio of coupling to field strength.

**Assumptions / limitations.**

The method assumes that short-time diabatic evolution can capture the ground state within a manageable subspace and that the heavy-hex architecture can be used to reach the quantum utility regime.

**Figures summary.**

Figure 1 shows lattice connectivity; Figure 2 plots average spin, energy, and information ratio for homogeneous models; Figure 3 shows similar metrics for random-coupling models; Figure 4 compares basis state probability distributions between exact and CVQE results.

**Paper structure.**

The paper introduces the CVQE algorithm and GSA, applies it to homogeneous and random Ising models on IBM hardware, performs error and entropic analysis, provides a mathematical framework for diabatic evolution, and concludes with scaling and utility assessments.

**Why it may be interesting.**

It provides a practical framework for using short-coherence-time devices to study many-body dynamics and ground states, specifically showing how to use diabatic processes to bypass the need for full adiabaticity.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We find the ground-state energy of the Ising model using the Cascaded Variational Quantum Eigensolver (CVQE) algorithm with the Guided-Sampling Ansatz (GSA) using up to 63 qubits on a quantum computer. We study a heavy-hex lattice to match the qubit architecture, allowing us to perform calculations in the quantum utility regime. We study both a homogeneous and random-coupling model. We locate the boundary of acceptable quantum errors as a function of the number of qubits and coupling strength. An entropic analysis is performed giving insights into the quantum computing performance. A subspace analysis is performed that suggests that the Ising model is especially suited for near-term quantum computing.

</details>

**Main problem.**

The study investigates the time-dependent dynamics of quantum memory and information scrambling (OTOC) in chiral spin chains, specifically looking at how broken inversion symmetry affects these processes.

**Main result.**

Quantum memory is found to be more sensitive to broken inversion symmetry and the Dzyaloshinskii-Moriya interaction than OTOC, exhibiting faster oscillations and a lack of equilibration. Additionally, a classical neural network can predict these dynamics, though its accuracy decreases with stronger DMI.

**Method.**

The authors use exact diagonalization and analytical derivations for single and two-excitation sectors, combined with a classical deep learning artificial neural network (ANN) trained via backpropagation to predict time-series results.

**Summary.**

This paper explores how quantum information scrambles and how quantum memory evolves in a chiral spin chain. By comparing the Out-of-Time-Ordered Correlator (OTOC) with a modified quantum memory formalism, the authors show that quantum memory is a more sensitive probe of broken inversion symmetry. They also demonstrate that a classical artificial neural network can be trained to predict these complex quantum dynamics, although the prediction error increases as the Dzyaloshinskii-Moriya interaction becomes stronger.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.**

A 1D chiral spin-1/2 chain (modeled after multiferroic materials or atoms on Ir(001) surfaces) featuring nearest-neighbor ferromagnetic (J1) and next-nearest-neighbor antiferromagnetic (J2) interactions, along with a Dzyaloshinskii-Moriya (DM) interaction term.

**Key observables.**

Out-of-Time-Ordered Correlator (OTOC), Quantum Memory (via Entropic Uncertainty Relations), von Neumann entropy, and multipartite entanglement (CKW monogamy inequality).

**Important parameters / regimes.**

Dzyaloshinskii-Moriya interaction strength (D), exchange constants (J1, J2), and the number of excitations.

**Assumptions / limitations.**

The analysis is restricted to specific excitation sectors (single and two-excitation) and periodic boundary conditions; the neural network is a classical architecture, not a quantum one.

**Figures summary.**

Figure 1 shows the experimental setup of an atomic chain; Figure 2 and 3 illustrate the neural network architecture; Figure 4 plots the time-dependent reduction in measurement uncertainty; Figure 5 shows OTOC dynamics.

**Paper structure.**

The paper introduces the problem of time-dependent quantum memory, defines the chiral spin chain Hamiltonian, presents the analytical and numerical methods for calculating OTOC and entropy, describes the ANN training protocol, and concludes with a comparison of the sensitivity of different observables and the predictive performance of the network.

**Why it may be interesting.**

This paper is relevant for many-body dynamics and open quantum systems because it explores how broken symmetries and chiral interactions influence the propagation of information and the stability of quantum correlations, providing a bridge between classical machine learning and quantum information diagnostics.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Entropic uncertainty relations are universal quantifiers of fundamental uncertainties of quantum measurements and are widely discussed in the quantum metrology literature. Quantum memory is a phenomenon related to the specific type of quantum correlations that allows for reducing fundamental uncertainties of quantum measurements. In the present work, the modified concept of quantum memory for time-dependent problems is proposed. We compare the time-dependent formulation of quantum memory with the out-of-time-ordered correlator (OTOC). Quantum memory is a rigorous mathematical concept that requires demanding calculations. Thus, until now, quantum memory has been discussed mainly for simple model systems and stationary problems. In the present work, we demonstrate that quantum memory can also be studied for realistic and physically relevant systems, e.g., the atomic helical spin chain, as well as the emergence and propagation of quantum correlations in time. We found that quantum memory manifests faster oscillations in time than OTOC and does not equilibrate. Furthermore, an artificial neural network is trained and asked to predict results for OTOC and quantum memory. These results show that quantum memory is more sensitive than OTOC in terms of broken inversion symmetry and the nonreciprocal effect.

</details>

**Main problem.**

Nanophotonic integration of silicon T centres causes spectral broadening and inhomogeneous distribution, preventing the mutual resonance required for entanglement protocols.

**Main result.**

The researchers demonstrated DC Stark tuning of up to 30 GHz in silicon T centres using p-i-n diodes, enabling 55% of emitters to be brought into mutual resonance and significantly increasing the joint excitation probability.

**Method.**

The study uses pulsed photoluminescence-excitation (PLE) spectroscopy, time-resolved single-photon detection, and second-order correlation measurements on silicon-on-insulator (SOI) nanophotonic cavities under cryogenic temperatures and applied reverse bias.

**Summary.**

This paper demonstrates the use of the DC Stark effect to achieve local spectral tuning of single silicon T centres in nanophotonic cavities. By integrating these centres into p-i-n diodes, the researchers achieved a tuning range of 30 GHz, allowing a majority of emitters to be brought into resonance. The study also explores the physical mechanisms behind linewidth broadening and luminescence quenching under high bias. This advancement provides a pathway to increase the yield of performant spin-photon interfaces for large-scale quantum information technologies.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.**

The system consists of single silicon T centres (solid-state defects) integrated into 1-D silicon nanophotonic cavities with p-i-n diode structures on SOI substrates, operated at cryogenic temperatures (1.6-2.5 K).

**Key observables.**

Zero-phonon line (ZPL) frequency, linewidth (homogeneous and inhomogeneous), photon statistics (g2(0)), excited-state lifetime, and luminescence intensity modulation.

**Important parameters / regimes.**

Tuning range of 30 GHz, cavity quality factor Q = 5500, temperature T = 2.5 K, and reverse bias voltages up to -120 V.

**Assumptions / limitations.**

Assumed that the observed spectral shifts are due to the Stark effect rather than Joule heating; TCAD simulations were performed at 300 K to avoid carrier freeze-out.

**Figures summary.**

Fig 1: Device architecture and T centre structure; Fig 2: Lifetime, photon statistics, and PLE spectra under bias; Fig 4: Intensity modulation and sigmoidal quenching; Fig 5: Energy level structure and splitting modulation; Fig 6: Modeling of joint excitation probability.

**Paper structure.**

Introduction to T centres and the challenge of integration; Device fabrication and architecture; Demonstration of Stark tuning and spectral control; Investigation of linewidth broadening and quenching mechanisms; Analysis of spin-orbit coupling and charge state dynamics; Conclusion on scalability.

**Why it may be interesting.**

This work is highly relevant to quantum optics and quantum networks as it demonstrates a scalable method for controlling the spectral overlap of solid-state emitters, which is a fundamental requirement for photon-mediated entanglement and quantum repeaters.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Among the many solid-state emitters being explored for scalable quantum technologies, the silicon T centre is a leading candidate offering long-lived spin qubits, a telecommunications-band spin-photon interface, and integration with on-chip photonic circuits. However, nanophotonic integration broadens both the inhomogeneous spectral distribution and individual emitter linewidths. Here, we integrate single T centres into silicon nanophotonic cavities with p-i-n diodes for local electronic control. These devices enable Stark tuning up to 30 GHz, sufficient to bring 55(2)% of on-chip T centres into mutual resonance, and demonstrate tunable lifetime reduction across the cavity resonance. A model of the joint excitation probability shows an orders-of-magnitude increase in entanglement rate by tuning distinct emitters into mutual resonance. Luminescence modulation at high reverse biases reveals a transition to a dark charge state. Finally, bias-induced modulation of the optical transition splitting uncovers a potential mechanism for electrically driven excited-state spin mixing via spin-orbit coupling. Localized and individual spectral tuning increases the yield of performant silicon spin-photon interfaces and the number of devices per chip available for large-scale entanglement and quantum information technologies.

</details>

### [Universal Topological Power Transfer with Arbitrarily Large Chern Number in Driven Quantum Spin Chains](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.24851v1)

**Authors:** Anshuman Tripathi, Mircea Trif, Thore Posske

**Type:** theory · **Category:** strongly correlated electrons · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.24851v1>

**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

**Topic relevance:** 🔥 `non-equilibrium dynamics` **4/5** · `analog quantum simulation` **2/5** · `exotic spin models in light-matter` **2/5**

(b) The self-intersecting torus traced by the drives for field strength B = 1/2. The critical points accumulate in dependence on the chain length N for both the odd and even cases. By choosing the magnitude and starting point of B, diverse topological pumps can be realized.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 4</summary>

<img src="2604.24851_figures/2604.24851_fig4.jpg" width="500"><br>

<sub>FIG. 4. A general frequency converter setup with distributed boundary driving and fine tuning to the degeneracy point. A wide variety of driving protocols can be engineered by apply- ing different combinations of static tuning fields to arbitrary sites to establish the degeneracy landscape, while subjecting other localized spins to the incommensurate phase drives.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 5</summary>

<img src="2604.24851_figures/2604.24851_fig5.jpg" width="500"><br>

<sub>FIG. 5. Scaling of the hyperbolic fit parameters with chain length N. (a) The parameter c of the new accumulated trajectories with increase of N, plotted on a logarithmic scale. (b) The parameter b (for N ≥3 as b = 0 for N = 2), plotted on a logarithmic scale.</sub>

</details>

**Main problem.**

Existing topological frequency converters are limited to small integer Chern numbers, which restricts the possible amplification and conversion factors of power transfer between driving fields.

**Main result.**

The authors demonstrate that in an interacting XXZ Heisenberg spin chain, the Chern number scales linearly with the chain length N, enabling a topological frequency converter with an arbitrarily large, quantized conversion ratio.

**Method.**

The study uses the Lanczos algorithm for ground state calculation, the Crank-Nicolson method for time evolution, and a gauge-invariant projection method to calculate the Chern number via Berry flux summation.

**Summary.**

This paper presents a theoretical mechanism for achieving arbitrarily large, quantized power transfer between two driving frequencies. By using an interacting XXZ Heisenberg spin chain, the authors show that the Chern number—which dictates the conversion ratio—increases linearly with the system size. This allows for a topological frequency converter with a tunable and potentially massive amplification factor. The study also identifies universal scaling laws for the quantum critical points that govern this process.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.**

An interacting XXZ Heisenberg spin-1/2 chain of length N, where the first spin is coupled to a static magnetic field and the last spin is driven by two incommensurate, circularly polarized magnetic drives.

**Key observables.**

Time-averaged pumped power (P), Chern number (C), ground-state fidelity, and energy transfer.

**Important parameters / regimes.**

Exchange anisotropy (delta/J), magnetic field strength (B), chain length (N), and driving frequencies (omega1, omega2).

**Assumptions / limitations.**

The system operates in the adiabatic or quasi-adiabatic regime, and the driving frequencies are sufficiently small to maintain the system near the instantaneous ground state.

**Figures summary.**

Figure 3 compares energy transfer and fidelity in adiabatic vs. diabatic regimes; Figure 4 shows a general frequency converter setup; Figure 5 illustrates the scaling of hyperbolic fit parameters (b and c) with chain length N.

**Paper structure.**

The paper introduces the problem of limited conversion factors, describes the XXZ spin chain model and driving mechanism, presents numerical results for Chern number scaling and degeneracy trajectories, analyzes the scaling of critical points using hyperbolic fits, and concludes with discussion on experimental platforms and limitations.

**Why it may be interesting.**

This work is highly relevant for many-body dynamics and quantum optics as it provides a scalable mechanism for high-gain, quantized energy/frequency conversion using topological protection in interacting systems.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Topological frequency converters exploit a quantized transfer of power between two driving fields in a quantum system, a phenomenon topologically protected by the Chern number of the associated fiber bundle. While realizations with few-spin systems have theoretically demonstrated this effect, the conversion factors have typically been restricted to small integer values. Here, we investigate an interacting $XXZ$ Heisenberg spin-$1/2$ chain driven adiabatically by two magnetic drives with incommensurate frequencies. The Chern number, determined by the degeneracy points enclosed by the adiabatic trajectory, increases systematically with the chain length and can be tuned through the exchange anisotropy, providing direct control of the topological pumping strength. We reveal a universal dependency of the anisotropy and magnetic field strength for odd and even chain lengths of the quantum critical points. This provides a mechanism for a topological frequency converter with an arbitrarily large, quantized conversion ratio in interacting quantum spin chains. The mechanism remains the same for arbitrary coupling regions of the drives.

</details>

The angle is θ = 35.5 ◦from (a)-(c) and 45 ◦from (d)-(f). The cSDW phase possesses the stripe-order in total occupancy while SDW phase has stripe-ordered variation in individ- ual components. Phase boundaries marked by red filled circles are obtained from mean-field decoupling theory [Eq. (15)].</sub>

</details>

**Main problem.**

Investigating the interplay between synthetic spin-orbit coupling and anisotropic dipolar interactions in 2D square optical lattices to understand the emergence of exotic magnetic quantum phases.

**Main result.**

The study identifies novel finite-momentum crystal phases, including phase-twisted and phase-striped supersolids and superfluids, and reveals a quantum phase transition at a magic tilt angle where off-site interactions vanish.

**Method.**

The authors employ a site-decoupled Gutzwiller ansatz and mean-field decoupling theory, supplemented by second-order perturbative analysis to determine phase boundaries.

**Summary.**

This paper explores how the combination of spin-orbit coupling and tunable anisotropic dipolar interactions creates complex quantum phases in 2D square lattices. By varying the tilt angle of the dipoles, the researchers found that the system can transition between checkerboard and stripe-ordered states, particularly at a specific 'magic angle'. The study also highlights the emergence of phase-twisted and phase-striped supersolids. These findings offer a roadmap for experimentalists using ultracold atoms to observe exotic density-correlated and magnetic phases.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.**

A two-component (pseudospinor) bosonic system in a 2D square optical lattice described by an extended Bose-Hubbard Model, including Rashba-type spin-orbit coupling and anisotropic nearest-neighbor dipolar interactions.

**Key observables.**

Superfluid order parameter, on-site densities, density imbalance, static structure factor S(k), momentum distribution, and magnetization.

**Important parameters / regimes.**

Spin-orbit coupling strength (gamma), dipole tilt angle (theta), hopping amplitude (J/U), dipolar interaction strength (V/U), and the magic angle (theta_M approx 35.3 degrees).

**Assumptions / limitations.**

The long-range dipolar interaction is truncated to nearest-neighbor interactions, and the single-particle hopping and SOC terms are treated as small perturbations.

**Figures summary.**

Figure 1 shows a schematic of spin-flip complex hopping and dipole orientation; other figures present ground-state phase diagrams for various SOC strengths and tilt angles, and illustrate phase boundaries for transitions between insulating and compressible phases.

**Paper structure.**

The paper introduces the physical system and model, details the theoretical methodology (Gutzwiller and mean-field), presents phase diagrams and results regarding the effects of tilt angle and SOC strength, and concludes with the implications for experimental observations.

**Why it may be interesting.**

It provides a detailed parameter space for observing novel finite-momentum crystal phases and supersolidity in ultracold dipolar gases, which is highly relevant for many-body dynamics and synthetic gauge field research.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We examine the two-dimensional spin-orbit-coupled bosons in the presence of an anisotropic dipolar interaction in square lattices. The spin-orbit coupling leads to finite-momentum superfluid and supersolid states, while the nearest-neighbour interaction induces crystalline characteristics in the quantum phases of soft-core bosons. We employ site-decoupled Gutzwiller ansatz and mean-field decoupling theory to obtain the phase diagrams and investigate the effects of the tilt of magnetic dipoles with respect to the polarization axis. Our study reveals the intriguing quantum phase transition of checkerboard finite-momentum phase-twisted and phase-stripe states into their stripe counterparts at a magic tilt angle, at which the off-site interaction along one of the directions becomes zero. At smaller tilt angles, the checkerboard charge-density-wave phase intervened by two compressible finite-momentum phases, and at strong spin-orbit coupling strengths, the phase-twisted supersolid and superfluid phases emerge. At larger tilt angles, a transition between the striped order of phase-twisted states and phase-stripe states occurs. The inclusion of off-site inter-component correlation leads to density-correlated phases, lattice-induced supersolid, and ferromagnetic quantum phases. Our study highlights novel finite-momentum crystal phases of spin-orbit-coupled dipolar bosons and provides a parameter space to observe them in quantum gas experiments.

</details>

**Main problem.**

Mid-circuit measurements (MCMs) and classical feedback latency/errors create significant bottlenecks in dynamic quantum circuits, leading to increased decoherence and branching errors in QEC and DQC.

**Main result.**

The MCMit co-design reduces feedback latency by up to 70%, improves circuit depth by up to 7x, and achieves up to 80% lower logical error rates in surface codes through improved state discrimination and hardware-software mitigation.

**Method.**

A hardware-software co-design featuring an FPGA-based controller with a multi-control branch instruction and neural network discriminators (Transformer and CNN) paired with software-level MCM elimination and stochastic branching.

**Summary.**

This paper presents MCMit, a holistic hardware-software co-design to mitigate errors and latency in mid-circuit measurements. By combining an FPGA-based controller with new neural network-based state discriminators and software-level error mitigation, the authors significantly reduce the overhead of dynamic quantum circuits. The approach improves readout accuracy for short durations and reduces logical error rates in quantum error correction. This is crucial for the advancement of distributed quantum computing and scalable fault-tolerant architectures.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.**

The implementation uses the Qubic 2.0 open-source framework on an Xilinx Alveo FPGA, evaluated using experimentally extracted readout traces from a five-qubit superconducting QPU and simulated surface code patches.

**Key observables.**

MCM error rates (bit-flips), readout fidelity, classical feedback latency, circuit depth, and logical error rates in surface codes.

**Important parameters / regimes.**

Readout durations (250ns to 2000ns), signal-to-noise ratio (SNR), and surface code distances (d=7, 9, 11).

**Assumptions / limitations.**

The evaluation assumes realistic noise models derived from IBM Heron processors and the effectiveness of the hardware-software co-design scales with the reduction of measurement-induced errors.

**Figures summary.**

Figure 1 shows the readout pipeline and neural network architectures; Figure 2 analyzes the impact of MCM error and latency on performance; Figure 6 illustrates the modified controller architecture; Figure 7 details the Transformer and CNN designs; Figure 8 demonstrates dynamic circuit simplification.

**Paper structure.**

The paper introduces the bottleneck of MCMs, proposes the MCMit co-design (hardware and software layers), details the implementation on FPGA, evaluates performance using experimental traces and QEC simulations, and concludes with a discussion on scalability and future directions.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Distributed Quantum Computing (DQC) and Quantum Error Correction (QEC) rely on dynamic circuits that include Mid-Circuit Measurements (MCMs) and classical feedback. These operations present a major bottleneck: MCMs suffer from high error rates that lead to real-time branching errors, while MCM and classical feedback latencies amplify decoherence errors. Current hardware controllers, qubit-state discriminators, and software error mitigation techniques fail to address these challenges holistically.   We propose MCMit, a hardware-software co-design to mitigate branching and latency-induced errors. MCMit introduces a scalable, constant-latency multi-control branch instruction for faster classical feedback and two qubit-state discriminators, a transformer, and a CNN, with high accuracy even under short measurement durations. On the software side, static MCM elimination and stochastic branching complement the hardware by mitigating residual branching errors that persist despite hardware improvements.   We implement MCMit on Qubic and evaluate it using experimentally extracted QPU readout traces. Our branch instruction reduces feedback latency by up to 70\%, improving circuit depths by up to $7\times$ over Qubic. Our CNN discriminator achieves 37-73\% higher accuracy for short measurement durations than the baselines, leading to up to 80\% lower logical error rates in QEC. Last, our software mitigation improves fidelity by 18--30\% over baseline methods.

</details>
