**Main problem.**

The paper aims to establish exact results for the Hubbard model on bipartite lattices in dimensions d > 1 by utilizing its full [SU(2) x SU(2) x U(1)]/Z2^2 global symmetry, specifically addressing the previously ignored hidden U(1) symmetry.

**Main result.**

The author establishes seven exact theorems regarding the ground state properties, including the absence of level crossings in symmetry-resolved sectors, the absence of quasiparticle double occupancy/empty sites, and the decomposition of spin-charge entanglement.

**Method.**

The study employs an exact quasiparticle representation via a u-unitary transformation and utilizes the Perron-Frobenius theorem and analytic perturbation theory to prove ground state properties.

**Summary.**

This paper provides a rigorous mathematical framework for the Hubbard model on bipartite lattices in dimensions greater than one. By identifying a hidden U(1) symmetry, the author establishes seven exact theorems that describe the ground state's behavior, such as the absence of level crossings and the suppression of certain charge fluctuations. These results offer a robust foundation for understanding electronic correlations in materials like graphene and cuprate superconductors. The work also demonstrates how the model reduces to the t-J model in the strong-coupling limit.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.**

The Hubbard model on bipartite lattices (such as square, honeycomb, cubic, BCC, FCC, and diamond) with transfer integral t and on-site repulsion U.

**Key observables.**

Spin S_s, eta-spin S_eta, tau-translational U(1) generator S_tau, energy and momentum eigenstates, and spin/eta-spin currents.

**Important parameters / regimes.**

Interaction strength u = U/t, number of lattice sites N_a, and the presence of external magnetic fields (h) or chemical potential (mu).

**Assumptions / limitations.**

The results are derived for bipartite lattices in spatial dimensions d > 1 and specifically focus on the regime of finite U > 0.

**Paper structure.**

The paper begins by identifying the full global symmetry and introducing a quasiparticle representation, followed by the derivation of seven specific theorems regarding ground state properties, and concludes with an analysis of the large-u limit and the mapping to the t-J model.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

There are few exact results for the Hubbard model on bipartite lattices of spatial dimension $d>1$. Nevertheless, the Hubbard model with transfer integral $t$ and onsite repulsion $U$ on bipartite lattices with $N_a$ sites, such as the square, honeycomb, cubic, body-centered cubic, face-centered cubic, and diamond lattices, provides the simplest toy model for describing electronic correlations in many condensed-matter systems and is therefore a quantum problem of considerable physical interest. Seven exact theorems that provide new physical insight into the model are established. Overall, the exact framework based on physical spins and physical $η$-spins for the Hubbard model on bipartite lattices of spatial dimension $d>1$ introduced in this paper offers a robust foundation for future studies of the model, as well as of the condensed-matter materials, such as cuprate superconductors, graphene and graphene-derived systems, and other quantum systems, that it describes.

</details>

**Type:** theory · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.25010v1>

[46] within a minimal three- level system basis that captures the essential physics of the phenomenon. The blue curve shows the exact dimer spectrum obtained from Eq. 9, while the orange curve corresponds to classical optical treatments (e.g., DDA/CPA/CES), which neglect contributions from ground- state vibrational excitations to the linear response by restricting each monomer to a single ground vibrational level. The red dash– dot curve shows the normalized monomer absorption of...</sub>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<sub>FIG. 2. Linear absorption spectra of linearly coupled quantum emitter arrays comprising ten emitters computed by exact numerical diagonalization. The details of the model and the parameters are presented in Section 3 of the Supplementary Information. The DDA/CES/CPA captures the orange features in the exact spectra of the emitter arrays, analogous to those in Fig. 1, which physically arises from Rayleigh-type transitions, as schematically illustrated in the orange box. In contrast, spectral features such as the first blue-shifted peak, displaced from the orange peak by precisely one quantum of the ground- state vibrational frequency, are entirely absent within the DDA/CES/CPA...</sub>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 3</summary>

<sub>FIG. 1. The ladder diagram [47] of the heterodimer illustrates the photophysical process in which the incident light field interacts perturbatively with monomer A alone. The double-line arrows denote processes directly mediated by the incoming laser. </details>

**Main problem.**

Investigating whether the linear optical spectrum of coupled quantum emitter arrays can encode nonlinear optical information, specifically Raman-type vibrational signatures, challenging the classical assumption that linear response depends only on individual linear susceptibilities.

**Main result.**

Demonstrated that inter-emitter interactions allow nonlinearities of individual monomers to emerge as vibrational sidebands in the linear response of arrays, even without cavities or permutational symmetry.

**Method.**

Theoretical framework using retarded Green's functions, Dyson expansion, and a diagrammatic approach (ladder diagrams) to identify photophysical pathways and compute exact absorption spectra.

**Summary.**

The paper shows that the linear absorption spectrum of quantum emitter arrays contains 'hidden' nonlinear information from the individual emitters. By using inter-emitter coupling, Raman-type vibrational features of monomers appear as sidebands in the collective linear response. This effect does not require cavities or high symmetry, making it a general feature of molecular aggregates and 1D arrays. This discovery bridges the gap between linear and nonlinear spectroscopy and enables new methods for spectral engineering.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.**

Quantum emitter arrays, including molecular heterodimers (e.g., Chl522-Chl520) and 1D/cyclic aggregates, modeled using a three-level vibronic Hamiltonian with electronic and vibrational degrees of freedom and dipole-dipole coupling.

**Key observables.**

Linear absorption spectrum (sigma(omega)), Raman-type sidebands, and vibrational combination bands.

**Important parameters / regimes.**

Excitonic coupling strength (J), vibrational frequency (omega_v), Huang-Rhys factor (lambda^2), electronic/vibrational decay rates (gamma), and exciton bandwidth (W).

**Assumptions / limitations.**

Assumes inter-emitter coupling is mediated by the electromagnetic vacuum; uses a truncated three-level basis per monomer and a simplified shifted harmonic oscillator model.

**Figures summary.**

Ladder diagrams illustrating zeroth, second, and fourth-order photophysical processes (Rayleigh vs. Raman pathways); plots of linear absorption spectra for heterodimers and 1D arrays showing emergent sidebands.

**Paper structure.**

Introduction of the challenge to classical optical frameworks; derivation of the Green's function and Dyson expansion; application to heterodimers to show Raman encoding; extension to 1D/cyclic arrays; comparison with classical approximations (DDA/CPA) and many-body approximations (TPA).

**Why it may be interesting.**

It reveals a way to probe hidden nonlinear/vibrational degrees of freedom using only linear spectroscopy, providing a new pathway for spectral engineering and quantum sensing in molecular aggregates and solid-state arrays.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Classical optical frameworks such as the discrete dipole approximation (DDA) assume that the linear spectrum of coupled quantum emitters can be computed solely from the linear susceptibilities of individual constituents. However, recent polariton studies show that cavity linear response can encode nonlinear optical susceptibilities. Here, we demonstrate that this phenomenon is more general: emitter-emitter interactions allow nonlinearities of individual emitters to emerge in the linear response of arrays, without cavities or permutational symmetry. To illustrate this phenomenon, we show linear spectra for coupled heterodimers and linear chains, and demonstrate that Raman features of individual monomers show up as vibrational sidebands of collective resonances. Moreover, tuning Raman-type anharmonicities enables systematic control of spectral features, establishing a genuine quantum optical effect in molecular aggregates and quantum emitter arrays, which goes beyond mean-field descriptions in light-matter interactions.

</details>

### [Kohn-Sham Hamiltonian from Effective Field Theory: Quasiparticle Band Narrowing from Frozen Core Dynamics](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.25199v1)

**Authors:** Xiansheng Cai, Han Wang, Kun Chen

**Type:** theory · **Category:** strongly correlated electrons · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.25199v1>

**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

**Topic relevance:** `Keldysh / 2PI / non-Gaussian methods` **2/5**

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

Mg along Γ–A–Γ: expt. = Ref. [5]. Al along Γ–X: expt. = Ref. [6]. PBE closely tracks LDA, confirming robustness to the functional.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<img src="2604.25199_figures/2604.25199_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>

<sub>FIG. 2. Dimensionless ratio f c K/∆Ec for the dominant core channel of each element. This ratio controls zcore [Eq. (3)]: elements with large |f c/∆Ec| have large corrections. K and Li have the largest ratios because ∆Ec is small (1.7 to 2.6 Ha) while |f c K| ∼Jc ∼1 to 2 Ha is comparable. The [Ne]-core se- ries Na–Mg–Al–Si shows monotonically decreasing amplitude: |f c K| is nearly the same for all four because Jc and the orbital size ⟨r⟩c scale inversely with Z, keeping |fc| ∼Jc⟨r⟩c ∼1 Ha; meanwhile ∆E2s roughly triples from Na to Si. The sign change near K ≈2 to 3 Bohr−1 leads to partial cancellation in the coherent sum F c νk.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 3</summary>

<sub>FIG. 3. Quasiparticle properties of the UEG at rs = 4 from DiagMC. Left: z(k) = 1/(1−∂ωRe Σ) is nearly constant within the Fermi sea. Right: [ϵk −∆Re Σ(k)]/ϵk (where ϵk = k2/2m and ∆Σ(k) = Σ(k) −Σ(0)) is likewise flat, confirming ∂ωΣ + ∂ϵΣ ≈0 and hence m∗≈m.</sub>

</details>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 5</summary>

<sub>FIG. 6. Li BCC band structure along N–Γ–H. Blue: KS; red: EFT QP. Solid: GTH-LDA (Zv = 1); dashed: EFT nonlocal KB PSP; dotted: PBE (Zv = 3).</sub>

</details>

**Main problem.**

The systematic overestimation of bandwidth in alkali and alkaline-earth metals by Kohn-Sham eigenvalues compared to ARPES measurements, caused by the neglect of dynamical core-electron excitations in standard DFT.

**Main result.**

The derivation of an Effective Field Theory (EFT) that introduces a renormalization factor (z_core) to the Kohn-Sham bands, successfully resolving the bandwidth discrepancy by accounting for frozen-core dynamics.

**Method.**

Construction of an EFT for the inhomogeneous electron gas using a dual-fermion approach and Renormalized Perturbation Theory (RPT) to integrate out core electrons.

**Summary.**

This paper addresses the long-standing discrepancy between DFT-calculated Kohn-Sham bands and experimental ARPES data in simple metals. By using an Effective Field Theory to account for the dynamical effects of core electrons that are usually 'frozen' in standard pseudopotentials, the authors derive a correction factor that narrows the predicted bandwidth. This approach successfully matches experimental results for several elements and demonstrates how LLM-based agents can be used to reconstruct and verify fundamental many-body theories.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.**

The system consists of sp-bonded metals (Li, Na, K, Mg, Ca, Al, Si) modeled via a Born-Oppenheimer action and a Kohn-Sham Hamiltonian augmented by an energy-dependent pseudopotential.

**Key observables.**

Quasipability bandwidth, quasiparticle residue (z), effective mass (m*), and the frozen-core renormalization factor (z_core).

**Important parameters / regimes.**

Core excitation energy (Delta E_c), core form factor (f_c^K), and the scale separation ratio (epsilon_F / Delta E_c).

**Assumptions / limitations.**

Assumes scale separation between core and valence energies, approximate Galilean invariance of the uniform electron gas, and the validity of the Born-Oppenheimer approximation.

**Figures summary.**

Comparison of LDA and PBE band structures with and without EFT corrections for various elements; validation of the Li BCC valence band structure using different pseudopotentials.

**Paper structure.**

Introduction of the bandwidth discrepancy; derivation of the EFT and the energy-dependent pseudopotential; application of Renormalized Perturbation Theory; numerical validation against ARPES and DMFT; discussion of the 'Agentic Science' paradigm for theory construction.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Kohn-Sham (KS) eigenvalues are routinely compared with angle-resolved photoemission (ARPES) and used as input for many-body methods, yet density functional theory (DFT) assigns them no physical meaning. For alkali and alkaline-earth metals, KS bandwidths overestimate ARPES measurements by 20-35%, a discrepancy that persists across all exchange-correlation functionals. We construct an effective field theory (EFT) of the inhomogeneous electron gas and show that two conditions imply KS bands are the quasiparticle bands, up to a frozen-core renormalization factor zcore: a scale separation between core excitation energies and the valence Fermi energy, and an approximate Galilean invariance of the uniform electron gas confirmed by diagrammatic Monte Carlo. This factor reflects dynamical core excitations that conventional pseudopotentials freeze out and no static potential can capture. The correction 1-zcore reaches 20-35% for alkali metals but falls below 5% for Al and Si, explaining both the failure and success of KS band theory. We derive a closed-form post-SCF formula and validate it for Li, Na, K, Ca, Mg, Al, and Si; the predicted quasiparticle bands resolve the long-standing ARPES bandwidth discrepancy, matching embedded dynamical mean-field theory at negligible cost. This work also exemplifies first-principles agentic science, a direction particularly suited to the AGI-for-Science paradigm: an LLM-co-developed derivation with controlled approximations, verified symbolically and against a few experiments, becomes a deterministic harness for agentic scale-out, resolving simultaneously the LLM audit bottleneck and the non-falsifiability of fit-based AI-for-science.

</details>

### [On the Mathematics of Information-Thermodynamics](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.24948v1)

**Authors:** Dallin Fisher, Qi-Jun Hong

**Type:** theory · **Category:** statistical mechanics · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.24948v1>

**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

**Topic relevance:** `entanglement & information structure` **2/5**

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2604.24948_figures/2604.24948_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>

<sub>FIG. 1: Heatmap showing the discrepancy between the asdf method and the analytic solutions for entropy of an ideal gas for 8, 000 ensembles with varying N and Λ. For real systems simulated with sufficiently sized cells, asdf is in good agreement with the analytic solutions.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<img src="2604.24948_figures/2604.24948_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>

<sub>FIG. 2: The entropy of a microstate Y can be approximated as the conditional entropy of the mapping between an uncorrelated microstate X and Y .</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 3</summary>

<img src="2604.24948_figures/2604.24948_fig3.jpg" width="500"><br>

<sub>FIG. 3: The information relayed from sender to receiver to construct a microstate Y given a relative microstate X can be constructed as procedural operations over a fixed sequence of the elements in X. For each element in X, information on how the receiver can build elements in Y must be supplied.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 4</summary>

<img src="2604.24948_figures/2604.24948_fig4.jpg" width="500"><br>

<sub>FIG. 4: Distribution of vector magnitudes r for raw and mapped (∆) data at varying system sizes for solid aluminum at T = 1000 K. The ∆representation produces a sharply peaked distributions near r = 0 with reduced variance, reflecting a concentration of residual displacements that is favorable for finite-precision encoding.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 5</summary>

<img src="2604.24948_figures/2604.24948_fig5.jpg" width="500"><br>

<sub>FIG. 5: Marginal coordinate distributions p(x), p(y), and p(z) for silicon in the diamond phase at 1400 K, shown for both raw and mapped (∆) representations. The transformation from raw to ∆coordinates removes the lattice-scale positional structure and recasts the system in terms of local displacements about a reference configuration. As a result, the periodic features present in the raw coordinate distributions collapse into sharply localized peaks at zero in the ∆representation.</sub>

</details>

**Main problem.**

The difficulty of computing thermodynamic entropy in interacting and complex systems where closed-form solutions are rare, and the need to validate an information-theoretic framework for entropy estimation.

**Main result.**

The authors analytically and numerically demonstrate that the asdf method accurately reproduces the thermodynamic entropy for the ideal gas and 1D harmonic oscillator, and successfully generalizes to complex condensed phases like liquids and solids.

**Method.**

The asdf method uses an information-theoretic approach that reformulates entropy estimation as the Shannon entropy of a residual mapping distribution between two decorrelated microstates.

**Summary.**

This paper validates the 'asdf' method, a new information-theoretic framework for calculating thermodynamic entropy from molecular configurations. By treating entropy as the Shannon entropy of a residual mapping between microstates, the authors show that the method can recover exact results for ideal gases and harmonic oscillators. The framework is shown to be robust for complex condensed matter systems, including liquids and solids, and can implicitly account for momentum contributions through proper discretization. This provides a powerful, compression-based approach to estimating entropy in large-scale molecular dynamics simulations.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.**

The study uses benchmarks including the classical monatomic ideal gas and the 1D harmonic oscillator, and extends to complex condensed matter systems such as aluminum, silicon, titanium, and tungsten in both solid and liquid phases.

**Key observables.**

Thermodynamic entropy (S), Shannon entropy (H), entropy of mixing, and thermal de Broglie wavelength.

**Important parameters / regimes.**

Particle number (N), temperature (T), thermal de Broglie wavelength (Lambda), and Debye temperature (Theta_D).

**Assumptions / limitations.**

The method assumes the separability of the Hamiltonian and that the momentum contribution can be recovered via a specific discretization scale related to the de Broglie wavelength.

**Figures summary.**

Figure 1 shows the convergence of the asdf method to the ideal gas analytic solution; Figure 4 illustrates the reduction in variance in the residual mapping compared to raw coordinates; Figure 5 shows marginal coordinate distributions for silicon.

**Paper structure.**

The paper begins with the validation of the asdf method against the ideal gas and 1D harmonic oscillator, moves to the generalization of the framework to interacting systems and mixtures, and concludes with applications to real material systems (solids and liquids) and the integration of electronic/quantum effects.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We present a validation of the asdf method, an information-theoretic framework for computing thermodynamic entropy from molecular configurations. The method reformulates entropy estimation as the Shannon entropy of a residual mapping distribution defined between two decorrelated microstates. We demonstrate analytically that for the closed-form Hamiltonians with known solutions, the classical ideal gas and the one-dimensional harmonic oscillator's entropy obtained from the compressibility of the residual mapping object reproduces the exact thermodynamic entropy. In each case, the conditional entropy of the residual mapping object with respect to an uncorrelated microstate is shown to coincide with the ensemble entropy derived from the canonical partition function. These results establish consistency between the asdf formalism and classical statistical mechanics for analytically solvable systems. We further discuss how the framework generalizes to interacting Hamiltonians. The analysis supports the interpretation of thermodynamic entropy as an information measure encoded geometrically in inter-microstate mappings and motivates application of the method to complex condensed phases.

</details>

### [Optimizing ground state preparation protocols with autoresearch](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.25610v1)

**Authors:** Luis Mantilla Calderón, Jérôme F. Gonthier, Ignacio Gustin, Varinia Bernales, Alán Aspuru-Guzik

**Type:** theory · **Category:** numerical methods · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.25610v1>

**Topic relevance:** `entanglement & information structure` **2/5**

The bottom row shows the corresponding distributions of stored post-equilibration block energies. </details>

**Main problem.**

Automating the optimization of ground-state preparation protocols (ansatz design, hyperparameters, and algorithmic choices) to replace manual, labor-intensive tuning.

**Main result.**

An LLM-based 'autoresearch' agent successfully mutated simple baseline protocols into complex, high-performing ones, achieving significant energy error reductions in VQE, DMRG, and AFQMC.

**Method.**

An evolutionary coding strategy using LLM coding agents (GPT-5.4) that generates code edits, executes benchmarks, and uses scalar energy proxies to iteratively improve quantum and classical simulation protocols.

**Summary.**

This paper introduces 'autoresearch,' an automated framework using LLM coding agents to optimize quantum simulation protocols. By treating ground-state preparation as a code-optimization problem, the agent iteratively mutates algorithms like VQE, DMRG, and AFQMC to minimize energy errors. The authors demonstrate that the agent can significantly outperform manual baselines within fixed computational budgets. This approach promises to automate much of the tedious parameter-tuning work in quantum many-body simulations.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.**

The study covers several systems: molecular electronic structures (BH, LiH, BeH2, H2O) for VQE; 1D spin-1/2 chains (Heisenberg XXX, XXZ, TFIM, XX) for DMRG; and small molecules (H2, LiH, H2O, N2) for AFQMC.

**Key observables.**

Ground-state energy error (relative to FCI or CCSD(T)), Rayleigh quotient, mutual-information error, and statistical stability (energy variance).

**Important parameters / regimes.**

Computational wall-time budgets, bond dimension, imaginary-time step, walker population, and optimizer hyperparameters (step size, tolerance).

**Assumptions / limitations.**

The effectiveness is evaluated under strict, fixed computational time and resource constraints; the scope is limited to small-scale proof-of-concept systems.

**Figures summary.**

Figure 1 shows VQE energy error evolution over iterations; Figure 2 compares mutual-information error matrices for spin chains; Figure 3 displays AFQMC energy traces and energy distributions.

**Paper structure.**

The paper introduces the autoresearch concept, details the agentic framework (gsopt), presents specific applications to VQE, DMRG, and AFQMC, and concludes with discussions on future scaling and automation.

**Why it may be interesting.**

It presents a novel way to use generative AI to automate the discovery of optimized numerical protocols for many-body physics and quantum chemistry, potentially reducing the human effort required for complex simulation tuning.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Artificial intelligent language-model based coding agents have significantly changed the way we interact with computers in our day-to-day, as it is common to use them to create, improve, and run programming scripts only using natural language. Agent code updates can be better guided when such programs can be executed and scored automatically rather than judged by human preference. In quantum computing and classical quantum simulation settings, ground-state preparation has a parallel structure: candidate protocols can be ranked by estimated energies and other proxies indicating proper quantum-state convergence. In this work, we study how autoresearch, a code optimization strategy based on coding agents, can be used to optimize hyperparameter choices of different ground-state preparation and sampling protocols, including the variational quantum eigensolver (VQE), density matrix renormalization group (DMRG), and auxiliary-field quantum Monte Carlo (AFQMC). We validate the viability and capacity of this method on simple spin models and molecular Hamiltonians. Across all three settings, the agent mutates simple baselines into complex protocols with improved energy proxies while operating under constrained space-time computational budgets. We conclude with discussions of other quantum routines that support executable scalar scoring, enabling evolutionary coding agents to automate a substantial portion of the protocol-tuning work that would otherwise be required manually.

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

<a id="paper-2604.25775"></a>

### [Pareto Frontier of Neural Quantum States: Scalable, Affordable, and Accurate Convolutional Backflow for Strongly Correlated Lattice Fermions](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.25775v1)

**Authors:** Yuntian Gu, Zeyao Han, Wenrui Li, Zhiyu Xiao, Tao Xiang, Mingpu Qin, Liwei Wang, Dingshun Lv

**Type:** theory · **Category:** numerical methods · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.25775v1>

**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

**Topic relevance:** `entanglement & information structure` **2/5**

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2604.25775_figures/2604.25775_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>

<sub>FIG. 1. Network Architecture and Efficient Local Updates. (a) Given an input configuration, a convolutional layer gathers information from local neighborhoods. The wavefunction is expressed as the determinant of the resulting backflow matrix. (b) SCALE enables efficient updates by only recomputing the orbitals of the affected sites. The computational cost of the determinant is reduced from O(N 3) to O(N 2) via the Sherman-Morrison-Woodbury formula.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<img src="2604.25775_figures/2604.25775_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>

<sub>FIG. 2. Time cost per optimization step as a function of system size. The plot compares the runtime scaling of SCALE against global architectures (ACE and Transformer) under a fixed batch size. SCALE achieves a highly favorable asymptotic complexity (empirically ≈O(N 1.65)), resulting in substantial speed-ups for larger systems compared to the steeper scaling of ACE (≈O(N 2.19)) and Transformer (≈O(N 2.90)).</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 3</summary>

<img src="2604.25775_figures/2604.25775_fig3.jpg" width="500"><br>

<sub>FIG. 3. Pair correlations Cp(x, 0) for the attractive Hubbard model. The plots show results for (a) 8 × 8 and (b) 12 × 12 systems at weak (U = −2) and strong (U = −8) interaction strengths, with t′ = 0. The results from our ACE and SCALE models perfectly align with the exact DQMC benchmarks, demonstrating their high accuracy. Statistical uncertainties from Monte Carlo sampling are smaller than the marker size in all panels.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 4</summary>

<img src="2604.25775_figures/2604.25775_fig4.jpg" width="500"><br>

<sub>FIG. 4. Accuracy vs. Cost trade-off on the U = 8 Hubbard model. The plots compare variational ground state energy against relative computational cost. (a) For the 16 × 4 (t′ = 0) system, we analyze the effect of progressively enforcing symmetries on the ACE architecture (purple stars). Labels denote the cumulative addition of point group (D2), spin-flip (σ), half-translation (T1/2), and quarter-translation (T1/4) symmetries. ACE achieves lower energies than Transformer [21], HFPS [23], and NNBF [25] while maintaining a fraction of the computational cost of the full-symmetry NNBF. (b) On the larger 32 × 8 (t′ = −0.2) system, the ACE and SCALE variants establish a superior Pareto front,...</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 5</summary>

<img src="2604.25775_figures/2604.25775_fig5.jpg" width="500"><br>

<sub>FIG. 5. Ground state density and spin distribution for the 16 × 10 t −J model with J/t = 1/3, t′/t = 0.2, hm = 0.5 at 1/8 doping under cylindrical boundary conditions. The color of the circles indicates the local hole density, and the arrows represent the magnitude and direction of the local spin density. We can see a clear filled stripe state from the results.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 6</summary>

<img src="2604.25775_figures/2604.25775_fig6.jpg" width="500"><br>

<sub>FIG. 6. Variational energy of ACE as a function of optimization steps. The system is a Hubbard model with size 16 × 4 and t′ = 0. We can see that symmetry projection greatly increases the variational accuracy.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 7</summary>

<img src="2604.25775_figures/2604.25775_fig7.jpg" width="500"><br>

<sub>FIG. 7. Ground state density and spin distribution for the 32 × 32 Hubbard model with t′ = 0 at 1/8 doping under PBC. The color of the circles indicates the local hole density, and the arrows represent the magnitude and direction of the local spin density. We can see a clear filled stripe state from the results.</sub>

</details>

**Main problem.**

The high computational resource intensity and scaling bottlenecks of Neural Quantum States (NQS) for simulating strongly correlated many-fermion systems.

**Main result.**

The introduction of a new Pareto frontier of NQS architectures, SCALE (for efficiency) and ACE (for accuracy), which significantly improve computational scaling and variational accuracy on large-scale Hubbard and t-J models.

**Method.**

Two new convolutional backflow architectures: SCALE, which uses a tailored design and Sherman-Morrison-Woodbury updates to reduce complexity from O(N^4) to O(N^3), and ACE, which uses a deep convolutional stack for maximum expressive power.

**Summary.**

This paper presents two new Neural Quantum State (NQS) architectures, SCALE and ACE, designed to balance efficiency and accuracy in simulating strongly correlated fermions. SCALE achieves a significant speed-up by reducing computational scaling through efficient local updates, while ACE sets new benchmarks for variational accuracy using deep convolutional stacks. The authors demonstrate the effectiveness of these methods on the Hubbard and t-J models, even resolving stripe orientation in large-scale simulations. This work provides a powerful new toolkit for exploring the physics of unconventional superconductivity and other complex electronic phases.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.**

Strongly correlated lattice fermions, specifically the 2D Hubbard model (including repulsive and attractive regimes) and the t-J model on various lattice sizes and geometries (cylinders and square lattices).

**Key observables.**

Ground-state energy, pair-pair correlation functions, spin density, hole density, and off-diagonal long-range order (ODLRO).

**Important parameters / regimes.**

Hubbard U, hopping parameters (t, t'), doping levels (e.g., 1/8-doped), and lattice dimensions (up to 32x32).

**Assumptions / limitations.**

The use of Green's Function Monte Carlo (GFMC) is subject to the fixed-node approximation; ACE's depth prevents the use of the efficient caching scheme used in SCALE.

**Figures summary.**

Figure 1 illustrates the convolutional backflow kernel and efficient local updates via the Sherman-Morrison-Woodbury formula. Figure 2 shows runtime scaling of different architectures. Figure 3 displays pair correlations for the attractive Hubbard model. Figure 4 shows the accuracy vs. cost trade-off and the Pareto front for different systems. Figure 5 visualizes ground-state spin and hole density.

**Paper structure.**

The paper introduces the computational problem, presents the two new NQS architectures (SCALE and ACE), details the algorithmic improvements for efficiency, provides benchmarks against existing methods (DMRG, Transformer-based NQS), applies the methods to physical problems (Hubbard and t-J models), and discusses the physical implications for stripe phases.

**Why it may be interesting.**

This paper is highly relevant for many-body dynamics and numerical physics as it provides a scalable way to simulate large-scale fermionic systems, potentially enabling the study of complex phase transitions and emergent orders that were previously computationally inaccessible.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Neural Quantum States (NQS) are now among the most accurate methods for studying strongly correlated many-fermion systems, outperforming existing many-body approaches for large systems. However, NQS calculations remain extremely resource-intensive. Here, we introduce a new Pareto frontier of efficiency and accuracy for NQS in simulating strongly correlated lattice fermions, defined by two complementary backflow-related architectures: the Sparse Convolutional Ansatz for Lattice Electrons (SCALE) (state-of-the-art efficiency) and the Accurate Convolutional ansatz for lattice Electrons (ACE) (state-of-the-art accuracy), benchmarked on the iconic Hubbard and $t-J$ models for large lattices. SCALE uses a tailored convolutional design enabling efficient local updates via low-rank determinant updates, reducing computational scaling from $O(N^4)$ to $O(N^3)$ in backflow methods and yielding a >40$\times$ practical speed-up in tests while maintaining high variational accuracy. As an application, we study the previously inaccessible 1/8-doped pure Hubbard model up to $32 \times 32$, finding no significant energy difference between horizontal and vertical filled stripe states - contrasting with half-filled stripe states when next-nearest-neighbor hoppings are included. ACE employs a deep convolutional stack to maximize expressive power, achieving unprecedented accuracy on large systems. Extensive benchmarks on Hubbard and $t-J$ models show SCALE delivers variational energies competitive with leading methods at a fraction of the cost, while ACE sets a new accuracy benchmark, surpassing recent results with only 1/6 the runtime for $16 \times 4$ systems. These new NQS approaches provide scalable, affordable, and accurate tools for exploring strongly correlated fermionic physics, such as the microscopic mechanism of unconventional superconductivity.

</details>

### [Quantum Dynamics via Score Matching on Bohmian Trajectories](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.25137v1)

**Authors:** Lei Wang

**Type:** theory · **Category:** numerical methods · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.25137v1>

**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

**Topic relevance:** `methods for driven-dissipative` **2/5**

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2604.25137_figures/2604.25137_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>

<sub>FIG. 1. Quantum dynamics as a continuous normalizing flow. The black curves are deterministic, non-crossing Bohmian trajectories, launched from particle positions sampled from the initial density and tracking the time-dependent densities shown by the blue waterfall. The blue waterfall plots the ex- act density |Ψ(x, t)|2 at evenly spaced snapshots, showing an initial Gaussian wavepacket splitting into multimodal densi- ties in a symmetric double-well potential.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<img src="2604.25137_figures/2604.25137_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>

<sub>FIG. 3. Training convergence on the d = 4 Morse chain over 45,000 epochs (raw per-epoch values, log scale). (a) Fisher loss vs. epoch. (b) Mean-energy error |⟨E⟩−Eexact| vs. epoch. ⟨E⟩is averaged over the M = 5000 particles and the trajec- tory time [0, T], and Eexact = 2.3236 is obtained from a 4D split-operator FFT reference.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 3</summary>

<img src="2604.25137_figures/2604.25137_fig3.jpg" width="500"><br>

<sub>FIG. 2. Training loop for the self-consistent score matching on Bohmian trajectories. The score network sθ determines the quantum potential Q, which together with the classical po- tential V drives the Bohmian trajectories via Eq. (5). Along each trajectory we co-integrate the deformation gradient F and its velocity counterpart G via Eq. (8). The Fisher diver- gence (Eq. (6)) measures the mismatch between sθ and the score target (Eq. (7)), and gradients dL/dθ propagate end- to-end through the trajectory via BPTT.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 4</summary>

<img src="2604.25137_figures/2604.25137_fig4.jpg" width="500"><br>

<sub>FIG. 5. Learned score field sθ(x, t) (streamlines) on the (x0, x1) slice of the d = 4 Morse chain (x2 = x3 = 0). Back- ground: exact slice density on a log scale. (a) t = 0: Gaussian initial state with approximately radial score. (b) t = π: an- harmonic coupling deforms the density and the score field. The learned streamlines remain aligned with the exact den- sity gradient across the wavepacket.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 5</summary>

<img src="2604.25137_figures/2604.25137_fig5.jpg" width="500"><br>

<sub>FIG. 4. Mean ⟨xi⟩(t) (a) and width σi(t) (b) for each of the four modes of the d = 4 Morse chain. Solid lines: exact FFT reference. Open circles: M = 20,000 particles propagated with the trained score network.</sub>

</details>

**Main problem.**

Efficiently solving the time-dependent Schrödinger equation (TDSE) in high dimensions, avoiding the exponential scaling of grid-based methods and the training difficulties of existing neural-network approaches like PINNs.

**Main result.**

The authors prove that a self-consistent score-matching objective on Bohmian trajectories recovers exact Schrödinger dynamics for nodeless wave functions and demonstrate high-accuracy density reconstruction in Morse chains.

**Method.**

A generative modeling approach that treats quantum dynamics as a continuous normalizing flow, using a neural network to learn the score function (gradient of the log-density) by minimizing a self-consistent Fisher divergence loss along Bohmian trajectories.

**Summary.**

This paper introduces a new way to solve the time-dependent Schrödinger equation by using neural networks to learn the score function of a quantum density. By simulating particles following Bohmian trajectories, the method reinterprets quantum evolution as a continuous normalizing flow. The authors prove that minimizing a specific loss function leads to exact solutions for wave functions without nodes. They demonstrate the method's success on complex systems like anharmonic Morse chains, showing that it can accurately capture quantum vibrations and density evolution.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.**

The method is tested on a 1D double-well potential for wavepacket splitting and a d-dimensional Morse chain representing anharmonic vibrations with a coupled potential.

**Key observables.**

Probability density (rho), score function (s), quantum potential (Q), deformation gradient (F), mean-energy error, and KL divergence.

**Important parameters / regimes.**

Morse chain parameters (D=12.5, beta=0.2, lambda=0.3); training with 5,000 particles over time T=pi with dt=0.01.

**Assumptions / limitations.**

The primary assumption is that the wave function is nodeless (rho > 0), which ensures the phase is single-valued and the score is well-defined.

**Figures summary.**

The figures demonstrate the convergence of the Fisher loss, the accuracy of the learned density compared to exact solutions, and the reproduction of physical observables like dipole oscillations and mode breathing in the Morse chain.

**Paper structure.**

The paper introduces the problem of TDSE scaling, presents the Bohmian/Madelung framework, proposes the score-matching/normalizing flow method, provides a mathematical proof of exactness for nodeless states, and validates the approach via simulations of double-well and Morse chain systems.

**Why it may be interesting.**

It provides a novel bridge between generative modeling (score matching) and quantum dynamics, offering a scalable, particle-based alternative to grid-based solvers that could be extended to many-body systems via fermion-flow architectures.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We solve the time-dependent Schrödinger equation by learning the score function, the gradient of the log-probability density, on Bohmian trajectories. In Bohm's formulation of quantum mechanics, particles follow deterministic paths under the classical potential supplemented by a quantum potential depending on the score function of the evolving density. These non-crossing Bohmian trajectories form a continuous normalizing flow governed by the score. We parametrize the score with a neural network and minimize a self-consistent Fisher divergence between the network and the score of the resulting density. We prove that the zero-loss minimizer of this self-consistent objective recovers Schrödinger dynamics for nodeless wave functions, a condition naturally met in quantum vibrations of atoms. We demonstrate the approach on wavepacket splitting in a double-well potential and anharmonic vibrations of a Morse chain. By recasting real-time quantum dynamics as a self-consistent score-driven normalizing flow, this framework opens the time-dependent Schrödinger equation to the rapidly advancing toolkit of modern generative modeling.

</details>

Addi- tionally, the mean is below the random threshold in 4 of the instances, meaning that the majority of sampled solutions are worse than random guessing. Consequently, solutions obtained for preprocessed...</sub>

**Main problem.**

The paper investigates the performance and scalability of quantum optimization methods, specifically Quantum Annealing and QAOA, for solving the Generalized Traveling Salesman Problem (GTSP).

**Main result.**

Quantum solvers achieve competitive solution quality on very small graphs, but both approaches suffer from a sharp degradation in feasibility and scalability as the number of nodes increases beyond 15.

**Method.**

The authors propose a novel QUBO formulation for annealing and a constrained QAOA pipeline using an XY-mixer, supplemented by a node-reduction preprocessing technique (extended NN2C).

**Summary.**

This paper evaluates how well current NISQ-era quantum hardware can solve the Generalized Traveling Salesman Problem. By comparing Quantum Annealing and a constrained QAOA approach, the authors demonstrate that while quantum methods can match classical performance on tiny instances, they struggle significantly with feasibility as problem size grows. The study identifies critical bottlenecks like the difficulty of maintaining constraints via penalty terms and the limitations of hardware embedding.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.**

The problem is modeled as an undirected, weighted graph mapped to an Ising Hamiltonian or a QUBO matrix. The experimental platforms include the D-Wave Advantage2 QPU for annealing and Qiskit Aer simulations targeting IBM Heron-class hardware.

**Key observables.**

Approximation ratio, feasibility rate (percentage of valid tours), solution cost, and embedding failure rates.

**Important parameters / regimes.**

Number of nodes (n) and clusters (K), QAOA layers (p), penalty factor (lambda), and variational parameters (gamma, beta).

**Assumptions / limitations.**

The preprocessing method assumes that reducing the node count preserves the essential structure of the optimal tour, and the QAOA implementation assumes a fixed shot budget and low-depth circuits.

**Figures summary.**

Figures illustrate the NN2C preprocessing method and show the distribution of solution quality (approximation ratio) and feasibility across various small and medium-sized GTSP datasets.

**Paper structure.**

The paper reviews existing TSP/GTSP quantum work, introduces the proposed QUBO and C-QAOA formulations, describes the preprocessing and dataset generation methods, presents empirical benchmarking results, and concludes with an analysis of scalability bottlenecks.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

This paper studies quantum optimization baselines for the Generalized Traveling Salesman Problem (GTSP), a clustered routing problem that naturally models variant selection and sequencing problems under discrete alternatives. We propose a novel GTSP QUBO formulation focused on maintaining feasible solutions for quantum annealing, as well as a hardware-executable gate-based pipeline utilizing the Quantum Approximate Optimization Algorithm (QAOA). We implement a constrained QAOA variant using an XY-mixer, which preserves the stepwise Hamming weight in the ideal circuit model, while feasibility with respect to the full GTSP constraints is tracked explicitly during post-processing. We compare the two quantum optimization paradigms on problem instances from GTSPLIB, an established benchmark dataset, and validate against classical state-of-the-art solvers. To mitigate current quantum hardware size limitations, we further extend a preprocessing method to reduce the node count in instance clusters, constructing new NISQ-friendly instances from reduced subsets. Across all tested instances, quantum solvers often produce competitive solution quality when tested on smaller graphs, but exhibit higher runtimes and a sharp degradation in feasibility and scalability as instance size grows. Our evaluation highlights where quantum optimizers can already succeed and which algorithmic bottlenecks, like sampling rates, runtime issues, and other practical failure modes, remain as open problems.

</details>

### [Quantum-Inspired Robust and Scalable SAR Object Classification](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.25755v1)

**Authors:** Maximilian Scharf, Marco Trenti, Felix Bock, Padraig Davidson, Tobias Brosch, Benjamin Rodrigues de Miranda, Sigurd Huber, Timo Felser

**Type:** both · **Category:** numerical methods · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.25755v1>

**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

**Topic relevance:** `entanglement & information structure` **2/5**

The region in the center, where the target is located, is left unchanged. <img src="2604.25755_figures/2604.25755_fig6.jpg" width="500"><br>

<sub>Figure 6 Comparison of the number of parameters in the model and the test accuracy of the compressed model with increasing compression threshold ε.</sub>

</details>

**Main problem.**

The challenge of achieving robust and scalable Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) object classification that is resistant to data poisoning and efficient enough for deployment on edge devices.

**Main result.**

Tensor networks, specifically Tree Tensor Networks, provide a way to compress models by up to 75% while maintaining high accuracy and offer a diagnostic tool via entanglement entropy to detect data poisoning.

**Method.**

The study uses Tensor Network Machine Learning (TNML) with a local feature map to encode data into a higher-dimensional Hilbert space, utilizing DMRG and Riemannian optimization for training.

**Summary.**

This paper explores using quantum-inspired tensor networks to improve the classification of radar (SAR) images. It demonstrates that these networks can be significantly compressed for use on small devices like drones without losing accuracy. Furthermore, it shows that tools from quantum information theory, such as entanglement entropy, can be used to detect if a model has been compromised by malicious data poisoning.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.**

The model uses loop-free tensor network geometries, specifically Matrix Product States (MPS) and Tree Tensor Networks (TTN), where the weight tensor is interpreted as a state of a many-body quantum system.

**Key observables.**

Schmidt coefficients, von Neumann (entanglement) entropy, and feature importance metrics.

**Important parameters / regimes.**

Bond dimension (chi), compression threshold (epsilon), and compression ratio (r).

**Assumptions / limitations.**

The study focuses on real-valued tensor networks rather than complex-valued ones and is limited to loop-free geometries.

**Figures summary.**

Figure 1 shows MPS and TTN graph structures; Figures 2, 3, and 5 use entropy to identify clean vs. poisoned pixels/background; Figure 4 shows poisoned SAR images; Figure 6 plots the trade-off between compression, parameters, and accuracy.

**Paper structure.**

The paper introduces the SAR classification problem, defines the Tensor Network Machine Learning framework, describes the application of quantum information tools for robustness and compression, presents experimental results on the MSTAR dataset, and discusses operational implications.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

SAR image classification naturally has to deal with huge noise and a high dynamic range particularly requiring robust classification models. Additionally, the deployment of these models on edge devices, such as drones and military aircraft, requires a careful balance between model size and classification accuracy. This study explores the potential of tensor networks to meet these robustness requirements, specifically evaluating their resilience to data poisoning. Unlike previous works that concentrated on conventional neural networks for SAR object detection, this research focuses on the robustness and model reduction capabilities of tensor networks in object classification. Our findings indicate that tensor networks are adept at addressing both the challenges of robustness and the need for model efficiency, thereby contributing valuable insights to the ongoing discourse in radar applications and deep learning methodologies in general.

</details>

**Authors:** Takashi Inoue, Shoji Yamamoto

**Type:** theory · **Category:** strongly correlated electrons · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.25401v1>

**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

The canonical basis vectors of a four- dimensional hypercubic lattice are projected onto four vectors, denoted e1, e2, e3, and e4, which serve as the primitive translation vectors for the two-dimensional Ammann-Beenker lattice. (c) Six types of vertices in the Ammann-Beenker lattice with coordination numbers z = 3 to 8. (d) The pro- jection window of the Ammann-Beenker lattice is a single octagon. The col- ors of the domains correspond to the vertices shown in (c).</sub>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<img src="2604.25401_figures/2604.25401_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>

**Main problem.**

The study aims to characterize the first-order (single-magnon) Raman scattering spectra in 2D quasiperiodic antiferromagnets via the spin-orbit mechanism and compare them to periodic lattices.

**Main result.**

In quasiperiodic lattices, Raman peaks bifurcate as exchange anisotropy moves toward the Heisenberg point, whereas periodic lattices remain singly peaked; the perpendicular-space representation of the spectra serves as a structural fingerprint.

**Method.**

The authors use linear spin-wave theory (LSW) with Holstein-Primakoff and Bogoliubov transformations, combined with a numerical analysis of finite clusters and perpendicular-space projection techniques.

**Summary.**

This paper investigates how the lack of translational symmetry in quasiperiodic lattices affects magnetic Raman scattering through the spin-orbit mechanism. It demonstrates that unlike periodic lattices, quasiperiodic structures exhibit peak splitting in their single-magnon spectra as the system moves away from the Ising limit. The study shows that these spectral features are highly sensitive to local coordination environments, allowing the Raman spectrum to act as a fingerprint for different quasiperiodic tilings.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.**

The system consists of spin-1/2 two-dimensional quasiperiodic antiferromagnets on Penrose, Ammann-Beenker, and Socolar lattices, modeled by a nearest-neighbor antiferromagnetic XXZ Hamiltonian.

**Key observables.**

First-order (single-magnon) and second-order (two-magnont) Raman scattering intensities, and site-resolved spectral weights.

**Important parameters / regimes.**

Exchange anisotropy (delta), exchange interaction strength (J), coordination number (z), and the spin-orbit coupling constant (lambda).

**Assumptions / limitations.**

The study assumes the spin-orbit mechanism involves an indirect electric-dipole coupling and utilizes the linear spin-wave approximation.

**Figures summary.**

Figures illustrate the geometric construction and projection windows of the Penrose, Ammann-Beenker, and Socolar lattices, followed by plots of Raman spectra, peak splittings, and site-resolved spectral weights mapped onto perpendicular-space domains.

**Paper structure.**

The paper begins with the geometric construction of quasiperiodic lattices, introduces the XXZ Hamiltonian and the spin-orbit Raman mechanism, presents numerical results for various lattices and anisotropy regimes, and concludes with a discussion on the structural sensitivity of the spectra.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We study first-order (single-magnon) inelastic light scatterings in spin-$\frac{1}{2}$ two-dimensional quasiperiodic antiferromagnets in comparison with those emergent on periodic lattices. Unlike second-order (two-magnon) Raman scatterings based on an exchange interaction between neighboring spins, the present observations involve an indirect electric-dipole coupling which proceeds through a spin-orbit interaction. We discuss the nearest-neighbor antiferromagnetic XXZ Hamiltonian on various quasiperiodic and periodic bipartite lattices. The first-order spectra, of our present interest, consist only of rotation-invariant and mirror-symmetric magnons, while the second-order ones cannot select any particular magnon. With the exchange anisotropy moving away from the Ising limit toward the Heisenberg isotropic point, every initial delta-function peak bifurcates or divides into more in each individual manner on quasiperiodic lattices, while it remains singly peaked all the way on periodic lattices. Such splittings depend on how many types of isocoordinated sites for each coordination number and relative positions between those of the same type. A perpendicular-space representation of the first-order Raman spectrum serves as a fingerprint of each quasiperiodic tiling.

</details>

### [Restoration of Ensemble Equivalence by Quantum Fluctuations](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.25837v1)

**Authors:** Alessandro Campa, Andrea Trombettoni

**Main problem.**

The study investigates whether quantum fluctuations, induced by a transverse magnetic field, can restore ensemble equivalence in a long-range interacting system that exhibits ensemble inequivalence in its classical limit.

**Main result.**

The authors find that ensemble inequivalence present in the classical Nagle-Kardat model is removed above a threshold transverse field value hc, where phase transitions become exclusively second-order.

**Method.**

The researchers use a Hubbard-Stratonovich transformation combined with a p-blocking approximation and a rescaling procedure to calculate the partition function and determine the phase diagram.

**Summary.**

This paper explores the impact of a transverse magnetic field on the thermodynamic phase diagram of a quantum spin chain with long-range and short-range interactions. It specifically addresses the phenomenon of ensemble inequivalence, which is a hallmark of certain classical long-range systems. The authors demonstrate that sufficiently strong quantum fluctuations can suppress first-order transitions, thereby restoring ensemble equivalence. By using a p-blocking approximation and a rescaling technique, they provide a way to map the phase transitions of the full model using computationally tractable finite-size blocks.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.**

A one-dimensional quantum spin chain, known as the quantum Nagle-Kardar model, featuring mean-field (all-to-all) ferromagnetic coupling, nearest-neighbor interactions, and a transverse magnetic field.

**Key observables.**

Average magnetization per spin and the nature of phase transition lines (first-order vs. second-order).

**Important parameters / regimes.**

Transverse field strength (h), mean-field coupling (J), nearest-neighbor coupling (K), temperature (T), and the blocking parameter (p).

**Assumptions / limitations.**

The study assumes that quantum fluctuations play a role similar to thermal fluctuations regarding equilibrium order and relies on a scaling property to extrapolate from finite p-blocks to the thermodynamic limit.

**Figures summary.**

Figure 1 illustrates the clustering/p-blocking method; Figure 2 shows the (K, T) phase diagram with first and second-order lines and tricritical points; Figure 3 shows the clustering of phase diagrams for different p values; Figure 6 demonstrates the successful collapse of rescaled curves.

**Paper structure.**

The paper introduces the quantum Nagle-Kardar model, details the Hubbard-Stratonovich and p-blocking methodologies, presents the numerical results for various p-blocks and h values, demonstrates the scaling/rescaling success, and concludes with the identification of the critical field for restoring ensemble equivalence.

**Why it may be interesting.**

This paper is interesting for many-body dynamics researchers because it demonstrates how quantum-induced fluctuations can fundamentally alter the thermodynamic stability and ensemble properties of long-range interacting systems.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We study the thermodynamic phase diagram of a one-dimensional quantum spin chain subjected to both mean-field and nearest-neighbor interactions, and to a transverse magnetic field $h$. The purpose is to determine the effect of the quantum fluctuations, due to the transverse field, on the phase diagram, in particular with respect to the occurrence of ensemble inequivalence. We denote our model as a quantum Nagle-Kardar model. To perform the calculation of the canonical partition function, we show that, due to the presence of the mean-field term, in the thermodynamic limit one can use the Hubbard-Stratonovich transformation in spite of the non-commutativity of the different operators appearing in the Hamiltonian, and we adopt a procedure of successive approximations that lead to the determination of the phase diagram thanks to a scaling property of the phase transition lines. The results show that the ensemble inequivalence, present in the classical Nagle-Kardar model, is removed above a threshold value $h_c$ for the transverse field. For $h$ larger than $h_c$ the phase diagram exhibits only second-order phase transition lines, implying therefore restoration of ensemble equivalence.

</details>

### [Sign Embedding Quantum Algorithms for Matrix Equations and Matrix Functions](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.25333v1)

**Authors:** Yanqiao Wang, Jin-Peng Liu

**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.25333v1>

**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

**Topic relevance:** `methods for driven-dissipative` **2/5**

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2604.25333_figures/2604.25333_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>

<sub>Figure 1: method overview</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<img src="2604.25333_figures/2604.25333_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>

<sub>Figure 2: shift-rotation and common scaling</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 3</summary>

<img src="2604.25333_figures/2604.25333_fig3.jpg" width="500"><br>

<sub>Figure 3: Roadmap for the three ordinary Sylvester regimes treated in Section 6.</sub>

</details>

**Main problem.**

Developing a systematic quantum algorithmic framework for solving various matrix equations (such as Sylvester, Lyapunov, and Riccati equations) and computing matrix functions (such as square roots and geometric means) in an operator-output setting.

**Main result.**

The authors introduce a 'sign-embedding' framework that provides explicit block-encodings for a wide array of structured matrix problems, achieving query complexity that is linear in inverse-conditioning parameters and logarithmic in error tolerance.

**Method.**

The framework uses a three-step pipeline: compressing the target operator into a matrix sign object, applying a logarithmic-sinc quadrature rule for rational approximation, and implementing the resulting shifted inverses using Quantum Singular Value Transformation (QSVT).

**Summary.**

This paper presents a unified quantum computing framework for solving complex matrix equations and computing matrix functions. By using a 'sign-embedding' technique, the authors can transform difficult matrix problems into a standardized form that is easier to implement on a quantum computer. The method is particularly powerful because it works for non-normal and non-diagonalizable matrices, provided certain spectral gaps are known. The resulting algorithms are highly efficient, scaling logarithmically with the required precision.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.**

The paper focuses on mathematical operators including the Sylvester operator (AX + XB = C), the Lyapunov equation, the Continuous-time Algebraic Riccati Equation (CARE), and matrix functions like the principal square root and matrix geometric means.

**Important parameters / regimes.**

Field-of-Values (FoV) gap, strip-resolvent bounds, error tolerance (epsilon), and conditioning parameters (mu, gamma, and kappa).

**Assumptions / limitations.**

The algorithms rely on the existence of a Field-of-Values gap or a strip-resolvent hypothesis to ensure error control and handle non-normal or non-diagonalizable matrices.

**Figures summary.**

Figure 1 illustrates the algorithmic pipeline from sign function to QSVT implementation; Figure 2 shows the transformation of matrices to separate their numerical ranges along the real axis.

**Paper structure.**

The paper introduces the sign-embedding framework, details the mathematical construction of the augmented matrices for various equations, describes the log-sinc approximation and QSVT implementation, provides complexity analysis for different regimes (FoV gap and strip-resolvent), and concludes with a comparative analysis against existing quantum algorithms.

**Why it may be interesting.**

This is highly relevant for researchers in open quantum systems and many-body dynamics, as the ability to efficiently solve Riccati and Lyapunov equations is fundamental to studying master equations, stability, and control in quantum systems.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We develop a systematic sign-embedding framework of operator-output quantum algorithms for matrix equations and matrix functions. Differing from the contour-integral treatment, we start with the matrix-sign embedding route: an augmented matrix $M$ whose half-plane matrix sign compresses the target operator either as a block of $\text{sign}(M)$ or, in projector form, through $(I-\text{sign}(M))/2$; we then construct a logarithmic-sinc approximation for the half-plane sign operator and combine it with structure-aware scaled multiplexing and nodewise rebalancing of shifted inverse families. For ordinary Sylvester equations, we offer an explicit block-encoding of the target matrix solution with query complexity linear in the inverse-conditioning parameters and logarithmic in the target error tolerance, under non-normal and non-diagonalizable settings given a field-of-values (FoV) gap or strip-resolvent hypotheses. These algorithms propagate the same overlap-based normalization bookkeeping to ordinary and generalized Sylvester equations, generalized Lyapunov equations, principal square roots and inverse square roots, matrix geometric means, and continuous-time algebraic Riccati equations (CARE). These results identify matrix-sign embeddings and nodewise rebalancing as reusable design principles for structured operator-output quantum linear algebra.

</details>

### [Six textbook mistakes in quantum field theory](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.24871v1)

**Authors:** Alexandros Gezerlis

**Type:** theory · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.24871v1>

**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

**Topic relevance:** `Keldysh / 2PI / non-Gaussian methods` **2/5**

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2604.24871_figures/2604.24871_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>

<sub>FIG. 1: Connected, momentum-space, second-order Feynman diagrams representing the S-matrix in a 2 →2 process (for a quartic interaction) in the s, t, and u channels.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<img src="2604.24871_figures/2604.24871_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>

<sub>FIG. 2: Contour of integration for Wick rotation, relevant to the 2 →2 process of Fig. 1 for the cases of: (a) k2 + ∆&gt; 0, and (b) k2 + ∆&lt; 0.</sub>

</details>

**Main problem.**

The paper identifies and corrects widespread conceptual errors and pedagogical 'muddledness' found in introductory Quantum Field Theory (QFT) textbooks and research literature.

**Main result.**

The author provides rigorous corrections to six specific themes, including the validity of relativistic quantum mechanics, the derivation of Noether's theorem, the proper promotion of fields to operators, particle localization, normal ordering, and the nature of Wick rotation.

**Method.**

The author uses a comparative approach, quoting specific textbook mistakes and providing mathematically rigorous corrections using canonical quantization, contour integration, and the Newton-Wigner position operator.

**Summary.**

This paper serves as a pedagogical critique of common misconceptions in Quantum Field Theory textbooks. The author identifies six specific areas where introductory texts often sacrifice mathematical rigor for simplicity, potentially leading to fundamental misunderstandings. By providing corrected derivations and physical insights, the work aims to prevent the propagation of these errors in the physics literature. It is particularly useful for instructors and graduate students seeking a deeper, more accurate foundation in QFT.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.**

The discussion focuses on relativistic scalar field theory (Klein-Gordon field) and scalar electrodynamics, specifically examining the transition from classical Lagrangian/Hamiltonian density to the interacting S-matrix.

**Key observables.**

S-matrix (scattering amplitude), Feynman propagator, probability density, and conserved currents.

**Important parameters / regimes.**

Mass (m), coupling constant (lambda), momentum (k, p, q), and the infinitesimal epsilon (i-epsilon prescription).

**Assumptions / limitations.**

The discussion is limited to the core aspects of QFT relevant to a first-semester graduate course and assumes familiarity with basic relativistic quantum mechanics.

**Figures summary.**

Figure 1 shows momentum-space second-order Feynman diagrams for a 2 to 2 process; Figure 2 illustrates the integration contour in the complex k0 plane for different pole configurations.

**Paper structure.**

The paper begins with a notation summary, followed by a systematic presentation of six thematic errors, each containing a textbook quote, a mathematical correction, and references to authoritative literature, concluding with a pedagogical reflection.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

This article discusses incorrect statements appearing in textbooks on quantum field theory (QFT); some of these mistakes also appear in the research literature. The focus is not on errors made by an individual author, but on conceptual muddledness that is widespread in introductory textbooks. We start from a bare-bones summary of QFT, meant to establish the notation. We then turn to our six paradigmatic themes, in each case quoting a specific example of the textbook mistake, a summary of material that is known to experts but is frequently mishandled in introductory works, pointers to authoritative references where the relevant concept is handled properly, as well as a concise correction that rectifies any issues. The goal of this work is to warn readers of the existence of several pitfalls and thereby stop these errors from further propagating in the literature on QFT.

</details>

**Main problem.**

Extending tensor network (TN) methods, which are traditionally limited to lattice models, to simulate interacting particle systems in continuous space.

**Main result.**

The developed TN framework accurately reproduces local observables and captures liquid-to-solid phase transitions in 2D hard-disk systems, offering a deterministic alternative to Monte Carlo methods with linear scaling of computational cost relative to system size.

**Method.**

A real-space discretization and cell-based coarse-graining scheme are used to map continuous particles onto an effective lattice model, which is then solved using TN boundary contraction via Matrix Product States (MPS) and Matrix Product Operators (MPO).

**Summary.**

This paper presents a new way to use tensor networks to study classical statistical mechanics in continuous space. By discretizing space into a grid and using a coarse-graining scheme, the authors transform a continuous particle problem into an effective lattice model. This allows for the deterministic calculation of absolute free energies and local observables, such as density and pair correlation functions. The method is shown to be highly efficient, scaling linearly with system size, and successfully captures the liquid-to-solid phase transition in hard-disk systems.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.**

The study focuses on 2D hard-disk systems with a pairwise repulsive potential, where continuous coordinates are mapped to a fine grid and grouped into cells to create an effective lattice representation.

**Key observables.**

Density, pair correlation functions, and absolute free energy density.

**Important parameters / regimes.**

Inverse temperature (beta), chemical potential (mu), lattice constant (a), and tensor network bond dimension (D).

**Assumptions / limitations.**

The method assumes a discretization of continuous space and uses a coarse-graining approximation where each cell contains at most one particle; the current scope is limited to 2D and short-range potentials.

**Figures summary.**

Figure 1 shows the mapping from particles to a coarse-grained cell model; Figure 2 illustrates the factor graph and TN representation; Figure 3 shows the unit cell structure; Figure 4 shows density convergence with bond dimension; Figure 5 displays spatially resolved densities in a finite box; Figure 6 compares pair correlation functions with GCMC; Figure 7 compares free energy convergence and computational scaling; Figure 8 demonstrates convergence to the continuum limit.

**Paper structure.**

The paper introduces the problem of continuous-space simulation, details the discretization and coarse-graining methodology, describes the construction of the tensor network, presents benchmark results against Monte Carlo simulations (density, correlation, and free energy), and concludes with scaling analysis and future directions.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Tensor network (TN) methods are well established for computing partition functions in statistical mechanics, though this use has traditionally been limited to lattice models. We extend the scope of TN methodology to interacting particle systems in continuous space. Through a real-space discretization combined with a cell-based coarse-graining scheme, we formulate an effective lattice model that explicitly preserves spatial locality. The partition function of this model is represented as a TN, and the thermodynamic quantities are computed via boundary contraction. We apply this framework to the two-dimensional hard-disk problem and demonstrate the strengths of the TN formulation compared to existing Monte Carlo simulations.

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

<a id="paper-2604.24847"></a>

### [The Classification of Pauli Stabilizer Codes: A Lattice and Continuum Treatise](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.24847v1)

**Authors:** Bowen Yang, Matthew Yu

**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.24847v1>

**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

**Topic relevance:** `entanglement & information structure` **2/5**

<img src="2604.24847_figures/2604.24847_page11.jpg" width="500"><br>

**Main problem.**

The classification of mobile Pauli stabilizer codes up to gapped interfaces and coarse-graining, and establishing the relationship between these lattice models and continuum framed TQFTs.

**Main result.**

The authors establish a bulk-boundary correspondence where the equivalence class of a Pauli stabilizer code is described by a Clifford QCA in one dimension higher, and identify qualitative distinctions between lattice and continuum theories regarding gapped boundaries.

**Method.**

The study utilizes algebraic L-theory, surgery theory, and homological algebra within a categorical framework of perfect chain complexes and quadratic functors.

**Summary.**

This paper provides a rigorous mathematical classification of Pauli stabilizer codes using the framework of algebraic L-theory. It establishes a fundamental link between lattice-based stabilizer codes and continuum-based topological quantum field theories (TQFTs). A key finding is a bulk-boundary correspondence relating stabilizer codes to Clifford Quantum Cellular Automata. The work also highlights how lattice theories can admit gapped boundaries in dimensions where continuum TQFTs cannot.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.**

The system consists of Pauli stabilizer codes defined by commuting subgroups of the Pauli operator algebra on a spatial lattice Z^n, specifically focusing on translation-invariant, mobile excitations.

**Key observables.**

Topological charges (point-like, string-like, and membrane-like excitations) and their braiding relationships.

**Important parameters / regimes.**

Lattice dimension n, Krull dimension of excitations (restricted to zero for mobile codes), and the prime p for qudit systems.

**Assumptions / limitations.**

The work assumes translation invariance and restricts the scope to 'mobile' stabilizer codes where excitations have Krull dimension zero.

**Figures summary.**

Table 1 provides a structural analogy between Pauli stabilizer codes, TQFTs, and manifold theory, mapping physical concepts like mobile excitations to mathematical concepts like n-morphisms.

**Paper structure.**

The paper introduces the algebraic framework for stabilizer codes, develops a classification using L-theory, establishes a bulk-boundary correspondence with Clifford QCAs, and concludes by comparing the results to the classification of framed TQFTs.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We classify mobile Pauli stabilizer codes up to gapped interfaces and coarse-graining using the framework of algebraic $\mathrm{L}$-theory. We compare this classification with that of framed TQFTs, theories that arise naturally in the continuum, highlighting a close structural relationship between the two. Our approach is formulated in the category of perfect chain complexes equipped with quadratic functor over the Laurent polynomial ring $R = \mathbb{Z}/p[x_1^{\pm 1}, \ldots, x_n^{\pm 1}]$, within which the collection of topological operators of Pauli stabilizer codes arise naturally as objects. In particular, we establish a bulk-boundary correspondence for lattice theories: the equivalence class of a Pauli stabilizer code up to gapped interface is described by a Clifford QCA in one dimension higher. This is done using the universal target category for stabilizer codes, which is the categorical spectrum whose existence and universal properties are introduced in this work. We conclude by highlighting subtle differences between the classification of Pauli stabilizer codes and TQFTs, leading to qualitative distinctions between lattice and continuum theories.

</details>
