Lower panel: The first IP differences in eV for the G0W0Γ29 data set evaluated w.r.t. experimental values. Comparisons in cc-pVQZ basis set are made for THC- scGW and all the vertex schemes discussed in this work. Experimental values marked * for respective molecular cases correspond to vertical ionization energies.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 8</summary>

<img src="2604.25581_figures/2604.25581_fig8.jpg" width="500"><br>

<sub>Figure 8: The convergence of the first IP value for water produced for all six THC-scGWΓ variants using different convergence thresholds. On the y-axis, we report the absolute differ- ence of IP when compared with the one obtained with 10−7 a.u convergence threshold. Both the x- and y-axes are in logarithmic scale.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 9</summary>

<img src="2604.25581_figures/2604.25581_fig9.jpg" width="500"><br>

<sub>Figure 9: The first IP values calculated by THC-scGW and six THC-scGWΓ variants for the GW100 set. All molecules are categorized into ten groups. The ∆CCSD(T) results were used as the standard reference. The dagger sign † showcase the failure of convergence in SOX. Horizontal lines represent ±0.3 eV error range from the reference values.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 10</summary>

<img src="2604.25581_figures/2604.25581_fig10.jpg" width="500"><br>

<sub>Figure 10: Imaginary part of the trace self-energy, ImTr[Σ(iωn)], for six selected molecules: ammonia, carbon oxysulfide, magnesium chloride, tetrafluoromethane, lithium fluoride, and krypton. Across these representative systems, the imaginary part preserves a robust screening-controlled ordering over Matsubara frequency.</sub>

</details>

**Main problem.**

The high computational cost of implementing fully self-consistent vertex-corrected GW (scGWGamma) methods for realistic molecular systems and the need to evaluate if Tensor Hypercontraction (THC) can accelerate these calculations without introducing significant errors.

**Main result.**

THC decomposition introduces negligible errors into scGW ionization potentials, proving it is a reliable acceleration route, though vertex corrections primarily cause systematic shifts in IPs rather than consistent accuracy improvements.

**Method.**

Self-consistent GW (scGW) and vertex-corrected scGWGamma using Tensor Hypercontraction (THC) for acceleration, incorporating various vertex approximations like SOX, SOSEX, and G3W2.

**Summary.**

This paper evaluates the use of Tensor Hypercontraction (THC) to accelerate expensive self-consistent GW calculations with vertex corrections. The authors demonstrate that THC provides a reliable way to reduce computational scaling without sacrificing the accuracy of molecular ionization potentials. They also find that while vertex corrections are important, they often result in systematic shifts in energy values rather than simple accuracy improvements. This work identifies THC as a key enabling technology for applying high-level many-body perturbation theory to larger molecular systems.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.**

Small molecular systems benchmarked using the G0W0Gamma and GW100 datasets, treated within a many-body Green's function framework using the Luttinger-Ward formulation in a grand-canonical ensemble.

**Key observables.**

Molecular first ionization potentials (IPs), electron affinities (EAs), and total electronic energy decomposition (one-body and two-body contributions).

**Important parameters / regimes.**

Interpolation-point ratio (alpha_Ipts), frequency grids (real vs. imaginary axis), temperature (beta), and chemical potential (mu).

**Assumptions / limitations.**

The study assumes the validity of the THC decomposition for the dynamic part of the self-energy and focuses on vertex corrections applied only to the self-energy level while keeping polarization at the GW level.

**Figures summary.**

Figure 1 illustrates Hedin's equations; Figure 3 shows logarithmic plots of energy convergence as a function of the THC interpolation-point ratio.

**Paper structure.**

The paper introduces the computational challenge of vertex corrections, establishes the mathematical framework of many-body perturbation theory, details the THC acceleration technique and various vertex approximations, presents benchmark results on molecular datasets, and discusses convergence and error analysis.

**Why it may be interesting.**

The development of efficient numerical scaling via THC for many-body Green's function methods is highly relevant to the advancement of large-scale classical simulations of many-body dynamics and electronic correlations.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

In this work, we benchmark tensor hypercontraction (THC)-accelerated fully self-consistent $GW$ (sc$GW$) and vertex-corrected self-consistent $GW$ (sc$GWΓ$) methods for predicting molecular first ionization potentials (IPs). The vertex function, $Γ$, is inserted into the self-energy in a fully self-consistent manner, and representative sc$GW$ and sc$GWΓ$ variants are assessed across the $G_0W_0\Gamma29$ and $GW100$ data sets. We find that the THC decomposition introduces negligible errors into self-consistent $GW$ ionization potentials, indicating that the acceleration preserves the underlying fully self-consistent results. Across both benchmark sets, vertex-corrected sc$GWΓ$ methods primarily produce systematic shifts in the IPs relative to sc$GW$ rather than consistent accuracy improvements. These results identify THC as a reliable route to lower-cost sc$GW$ and sc$GWΓ$ calculations

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

<a id="paper-2604.25042"></a>

### [Stabilizers for Compiling Logical Circuits under Hardware Constraints](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.25042v1)

**Authors:** Jack Weinberg, Narayanan Rengaswamy

**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.25042v1>

**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2604.25042_figures/2604.25042_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>

<sub>Fig. 1. Toy Connectivity Graph</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<img src="2604.25042_figures/2604.25042_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>

<sub>Fig. 2. Non-Trivial Connectivity Graph</sub>

</details>

**Main problem.**

How to compile arbitrary logical quantum gates on hardware with restricted connectivity and limited accessible Hamiltonians by leveraging the redundancy of error-correcting codes.

**Main result.**

The authors provide a closed-form solution to the compilation problem by formulating it as a least-squares optimization over the stabilizer subalgebra, allowing for the selection of physical Hamiltonians that are easier to implement.

**Method.**

The problem is reduced to a least-squares minimization problem using the Moore-Penrose pseudoinverse within the framework of Lie algebra (su(N)) and the adjoint action.

**Summary.**

This paper presents a novel method for quantum compilation that utilizes the redundancy in error-correcting codes to find physical implementations of logical gates that respect hardware constraints. By treating the choice of stabilizer as a least-squares optimization problem, the authors show how to avoid expensive operations like SWAP gates by instead using naturally accessible physical Hamiltonians. The framework is general for any unitary in the SU(N) group and provides a mathematically precise way to bridge the gap between logical algorithms and physical hardware connectivity.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.**

The framework applies to quantum error-correcting codes (specifically the [[4, 2, 2]] code) and hardware architectures with restricted connectivity, such as superconducting, trapped ion, or neutral atom platforms.

**Key observables.**

The distance (error) between the target logical Hamiltonian and the accessible physical Hamiltonian, and the fidelity of the implemented unitary.

**Important parameters / regimes.**

The connectivity graph of the hardware, the specific error-correcting code used, and the set of accessible Lie algebra generators.

**Assumptions / limitations.**

The method assumes a fixed error-correcting code and encoding circuit, a predefined set of accessible Hamiltonians, and that the hardware allows for the implementation of the resulting physical Hamiltonian.

**Figures summary.**

Figure 1 shows a toy connectivity graph used for the CNOT implementation example; Figure 2 displays a non-trivial connectivity graph for a 4-qubit system.

**Paper structure.**

The paper introduces the dual problem of fault-tolerance and compilation, develops a mathematical framework using Lie theory to define logical and stabilizer subalgebras, formulates the compilation as a least-squares optimization, provides a closed-form solution via the pseudoinverse, and demonstrates the approach using the [[4, 2, 2]] code.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

To implement quantum algorithms on a quantum computer, we must overcome the twin problems of fault-tolerance -- how can we realize a relatively noiseless computation by cleverly combining noisy components? -- and compilation -- how can we realize an arbitrary quantum algorithm given the basic operations available on the quantum device at hand? We show how treating the former problem via error-correcting codes enables greater flexibility in resolving the latter. Specifically, we explicitly leverage the fact that error-correcting codes introduce redundancy which renders physically distinct operators logically indistinguishable. In terms of computation, it suffices to implement any operator logically equivalent to some target, yet from a compilation perspective, certain choices may be preferable to others. Our novel contribution is making this intuition precise in the general setting of the special unitary group. In particular, we describe how to reduce the problem of making a compilation-ideal choice to a least squares problem and provide a closed form solution thereof. Using our framework, it is possible to circumvent inserting costly swaps to adhere to hardware connectivity; instead, we could realize the logical target through a distinct physical Hamiltonian that is natively accessible. We elucidate our approach using the $[[4,2,2]]$ code. We discuss connections to compressed sensing that may pave the way to efficient compilation leveraging physical degrees of freedom.

</details>

**Main problem.**

The study investigates how odd-parity altermagnetism (ALM) reconstructs the momentum-space topology, edge states, and Hall transport in the presence of electronic interactions in a Chern insulator.

**Main result.**

The transition from a conventional Chern-insulating phase to an ALM-induced phase redistributes Berry curvature and alters edge state symmetry without changing the total Chern number, while maintaining quantized low-frequency Hall conductivity.

**Method.**

The authors employ the cluster slave-spin method, utilizing a 2-site cluster with exact diagonalization and a mean-field decoupling approach to treat strong electronic correlations.

**Summary.**

This paper explores how odd-parity altermagnetism affects the topological properties of the Haldane-Hubbard model. It demonstrates that while the global Chern number remains invariant during the transition to an altermagnetic Chern-insulating phase, the local Berry curvature is significantly redistributed. This reconstruction leads to spin-valley-locked edge states in zigzag ribbons and specific signatures in the optical Hall response. The findings establish the Haldane-Hubbard model as a key platform for studying the interplay of symmetry, interactions, and topology.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.**

The Haldane-Hubbard model on a hexagonal lattice, which includes nearest-neighbor hopping, next-nearest-neighbor Haldane hopping, and onsite Coulomb repulsion.

**Key observables.**

Berry curvature, edge state spectra (zigzag and armchair ribbons), Chern number, and optical conductivity (longitudinal and transverse).

**Important parameters / regimes.**

Hubbard interaction strength (U), Haldane hopping (lambda), and chemical potential (mu).

**Assumptions / limitations.**

The use of a 2-site cluster slave-spin approximation and a mean-field treatment for the coupling between the cluster and its environment.

**Figures summary.**

Fig 1 shows the HH model ground state and the slave-spin approximation scheme; Fig 2 presents the phase diagram and spinon energy dispersion; Fig 3 illustrates spin-resolved Berry curvature across different interaction regimes; Fig 4 details the evolution of zigzag edge spectra and LDOS.

**Paper structure.**

The paper introduces the problem of odd-parity ALM in correlated phases, describes the HH model and slave-spin method, presents the phase diagram and Berry curvature redistribution, analyzes edge state evolution in different ribbon geometries, discusses optical response, and concludes with experimental implications.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Odd-parity altermagnetism(ALM) extends compensated collinear magnetism beyond the even-parity spin splitting of conventional altermagnets, but its role in correlated topological phases remains largely unexplored. Using the cluster slave-spin method, we show that the odd-parity ALM appearing in the ALM Chern-insulating phase of Haldane-Hubbard model significantly reconstructs the local topology in the conventional Chern-insulating phase, while the total Chern number remains unchanged compared to the Chern-insulating phase. The Berry curvature becomes spin and valley selective; zigzag ribbons develop chiral-symmetry-breaking edge states; while armchair ribbons remain inversion symmetric. The optical response mirrors this separation between the local reconstruction and the global topology: low-energy spectra are governed by quasiparticles near the gap, whereas the low-frequency Hall conductivity stays quantized, $σ_{\rm T\uparrow}(Ω\to 0)=σ_{\rm T\downarrow}(Ω\to 0)=e^2/h$. These results establish the Haldane-Hubbard model as a minimal correlated platform for odd-parity altermagnetic topology.

</details>

### [Theory of Anderson localization on the hyperbolic plane](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.24917v1)

**Authors:** Alexander Altland, Tobias Micklitz, Devasheesh Sharma, Maksimilian Usoltcev, Carolin Wille

**Type:** theory · **Category:** disordered systems and neural networks · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.24917v1>

**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

In this parameter domain, the cutoff u(ℓ) ∼1 below which loop interference becomes ineffec- tive is reached at finite residual values of the conduc- tivity. The boundary to the localized regime is marked by a separatrix, σsep(u) defined by the condition that σsep(u →1) ≲1, i.e. a conductivity suppressed down to small values at u ∼1. Finally, note that the β-function defined by the right-hand...</sub>

The curves are scaled manually to a coinciding lowest mode value. (The lat- ter is finite, and determined by the effective curvature whose detailed comparison between different discretizations is not straightforward.) We notice strong deviations between the continuum and both Bethe, and Husimi cactus discretizations. The regular {7, 3} tessellation shows much better agreement, with, however, increasing deviations beyond the lowest lying modes. As expected, the PD...</sub>

</details>

**Main problem.**

Developing a unified theoretical framework for Anderson localization on the hyperbolic plane that interpolates between 2D-like behavior at short scales and infinite-dimensional behavior at large scales.

**Main result.**

The derivation of a two-parameter renormalization group flow for conductivity and curvature, identifying a critical line (separatrix) that separates metallic and insulating phases.

**Method.**

A combination of a non-linear sigma-model using the supersymmetry formalism, renormalization group analysis, and lattice discretization using Poisson-Delaunay random graphs.

**Summary.**

This paper provides a complete theory of Anderson localization on a surface with negative curvature. It explains how the geometry of the hyperbolic plane causes a crossover from standard 2D localization to a high-dimensional regime where metallicity can be protected. By using both field theory and graph-based models, the authors derive a unified renormalization group flow that predicts a true phase transition between metallic and insulating states.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.**

The two-dimensional hyperbolic plane (H2) with constant negative curvature, modeled via a Hamiltonian with a delta-correlated random potential and discretized using various graph structures like Bethe lattices and Poisson-Delaunay graphs.

**Key observables.**

Scale-dependent conductivity (sigma), scale-dependent curvature (u), resistance (R), and critical exponents (nu and beta).

**Important parameters / regimes.**

Disorder strength (W), curvature radius (L), conductivity (sigma), and the scaling variable (u).

**Assumptions / limitations.**

One-loop approximation in the renormalization group integration; use of a delta-correlated random potential; assumption that the Poisson-Delaunay discretization accurately captures the continuum spectrum in the high-density limit.

**Figures summary.**

Figure 4 shows a generation of a cactus graph; Figure 5 is mentioned in the context of discretization/graph properties.

**Paper structure.**

The paper begins with a continuum sigma-model to establish the 2D-like localization physics, moves to a lattice discretization (Bethe lattice and random graphs) to capture the high-dimensional transition, and concludes by deriving the coupled RG flow equations and the critical line.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

The two-dimensional hyperbolic plane, $\mathbb{H}^2$, is an unusual system in that dimensionality changes with scale: locally two-dimensional and planar at short distances, but effectively infinite-dimensional at large scales, it provides an interesting paradigm for the study of (quantum) phase transitions, notably the disorder-driven Anderson transition. Generalizing previous work, which treated short and large distance scales separately, we develop a unified framework interpolating between the principles of low- and high-dimensional Anderson localization. As a main result, we derive a two-parameter flow in a plane spanned by scale-dependent curvature (setting the system's effective dimensionality) and conductivity, with an extended critical line separating metallic and insulating phases.

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

### [Thermodynamic Identification of the Internal Superconducting Phase Boundary in UTe$_2$ for $H \parallel b$](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.25896v1)

**Authors:** Michal Vališka, Tetiana Haidamak, Andrej Cabala, Petr Proschek, Andreas Hausprug, Sergei Zherlitsyn, Vladimír Sechovský

**Type:** experiment · **Category:** strongly correlated electrons · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.25896v1>

The mode selectivity also has symmetry implications. In orthorhombic D2h symmetry, diﬀerent shear modes probe diﬀerent strain channels: C44 couples to εbc, C55 to εac, and C66 to εab [27, 28]. Recent ultrasound work argued that the low-ﬁeld superconducting state is single- component, with an odd-parity B2u state as the most compatible candidate, based on the absence of shear- modulus discontinuities at Tc [28]. In this context, the ﬁnite anomaly in C44, together with the absence of a resolvable C55...</sub>

</details>

<img src="2604.25896_figures/2604.25896_fig3.jpg" width="500"><br>

<sub>FIG. 3. H–T phase diagram of UTe2 for H ∥b. Filled symbols denote phase boundaries determined in the present work, and open symbols indicate literature data from ac- susceptibility measurements by Sakai et al. [11]. The ultrasound-resolved internal boundary remains nearly con- stant in ﬁeld near 14 T and terminates near 13.5 T and 1.25 K at a tetracritical point (TetCP), completing the local phase- diagram topology by providing the fourth phase line required at the TetCP. The superconducting regions SC1, SC1+SC2, and SC2 are indicated.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 4</summary>

<img src="2604.25896_figures/2604.25896_fig4.jpg" width="500"><br>

<sub>FIG. 2. Temperature dependence of the elastic response for selected magnetic ﬁelds. (a) C33(T ) measured with q ∥c and u ∥c. (b) C44(T ) measured with q ∥c and u ∥b. (c) C55(T ) measured with q ∥c and u ∥a. Each panel includes the 0 and 18 T curves, and the marked anomalies denote transition points used to construct the phase diagram in Fig. 3. Vertical scales are indicated by the scale bars in each panel.</sub>

</details>

**Main problem.**

The existence of an unresolved internal superconducting phase boundary in UTe2 for magnetic fields along the b-axis lacked definitive bulk thermodynamic evidence.

**Main result.**

Ultrasound measurements provide the first bulk thermodynamic evidence for this internal phase boundary, identifying a tetracritical point near 13.5 T and 1.25 K and revealing symmetry-selective coupling to lattice strain.

**Method.**

Pulse-echo ultrasound spectroscopy of various elastic modes (C33, C44, C55) and transverse magnetostriction measurements.

**Summary.**

This paper provides the first thermodynamic confirmation of an internal superconducting phase boundary in UTe2 for magnetic fields applied along the b-axis. Using ultrasound spectroscopy, the authors observed a significant softening in the C33 elastic mode and a kink in magnetostriction at approximately 14 T. They identified a tetracritical point where the phase boundary terminates and demonstrated that the anomaly is symmetry-selective. This discovery confirms the boundary is a bulk thermodynamic feature and supports the existence of multicomponent superconductivity in this material.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.**

The physical system is UTe2, an orthorhombic heavy-fermion paramagnet and unconventional superconductor, studied in high magnetic fields (up to 18 T) and low temperatures (down to 0.33 K).

**Key observables.**

Elastic constants (C33, C44, C55), relative frequency shift (delta f/f), and transverse magnetostriction (delta lc/lc).

**Important parameters / regimes.**

Magnetic field H parallel to the b-axis, temperature down to 0.33 K, and the phase boundary near 14-15 T.

**Assumptions / limitations.**

The study assumes the use of ultraclean single crystals to ensure the observed features are intrinsic bulk properties.

**Figures summary.**

Figure 1 shows the field dependence of elastic responses: (a) C33 softening and magnetostriction kink at 14 T, (b) C44 hardening anomaly, and (c) no resolvable anomaly in C55.

**Paper structure.**

The paper introduces the unresolved phase boundary problem, describes the experimental setup using ultrasound and magnetostriction, presents the identification of the thermodynamic boundary via elastic anomalies, discusses the symmetry-selective coupling and the tetracritical point, and concludes with implications for the order-parameter structure.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

The $H$--$T$ phase diagram of UTe$_2$ for magnetic field along the hard $b$ axis contains an unresolved internal boundary near $μ_0H \sim 14$--15~T, previously inferred from ac susceptibility and transport experiments but lacking thermodynamic evidence. We report ultrasound results for several elastic modes in an ultraclean UTe$_2$ single crystal with $T_c>2$~K for $H \parallel b$ down to 0.33~K and up to 18~T. A pronounced anomaly in the longitudinal $C_{33}$ mode, with a weaker response in $C_{44}$ and no resolvable anomaly in $C_{55}$, establishes this feature as a bulk thermodynamic phase boundary and reveals a symmetry-selective coupling to lattice strain. The phase line remains nearly constant in field near 14~T and terminates near 13.5~T and 1.25~K at a tetracritical point, providing the thermodynamic evidence for the fourth phase boundary in the $H$--$T$ phase diagram. The results constrain the order-parameter structure of the high-field phase and support field-induced multicomponent superconductivity in UTe$_2$.

</details>

**Authors:** Hasan Yilmaz, Masahiko Isobe, Oliver Clemens, Pascal Puphal

**Type:** experiment · **Category:** strongly correlated electrons · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.25575v1>

(b) SEM-BSE im- ages of cross-sectioned single crystals of the same as grown La2NiO4.15, reduced La2NiO4 and after PTFE fluorination. (c) Corresponding fluorine (F Kα) elemental maps on a PTFE treated specimen, highlighting pronounced fluorine accumu- lation near the crystal surface (marked by red ellipses), in- dicative of surface-dominated fluorination (d) EDX line scan acquired across the crystal cross section of a cleaved crystal over, as well as a magnified view, of the near-surface...</sub>

**Main problem.**

The study addresses the lack of systematic research on the topochemical fluorination of bulk single crystals of La2NiO4+d, aiming to understand the intrinsic structure-property relationships and the mechanism of fluorine incorporation.

**Main result.**

The researchers discovered that topochemical fluorination preserves the Ruddlesden-Popper framework while inducing a previously unreported fluorine-intercalated superstructure (C2/m symmetry) and altering the antiferromagnetic ordering.

**Method.**

Single crystals were grown via the optical floating zone method and subjected to fluorination using agents like PTFE, PVDF, and CuF2. Characterization included SC-XRD, SEM-EDX, TGA, and magnetic susceptibility measurements.

**Summary.**

This paper investigates the topochemical fluorination of La2NiO4+d single crystals using various fluorine-containing agents. It demonstrates that fluorine can be intercalated into the rock-salt layers of the Ruddlesden-Popper structure without destroying the crystal framework. The study reveals the emergence of a new monoclinic superstructure and changes in the material's magnetic properties. This work provides a new route for tuning the electronic and structural properties of nickelates in bulk form.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.**

The physical system consists of La2NiO4+d (Ruddlesden-Popper n=1) single crystals, including their topochemically reduced (La2NiO4) and fluorinated (La2NiO4-xF2x) variants.

**Key observables.**

Lattice parameters, space group symmetry, fluorine incorporation degree, Ni valence states, Neel temperature (TN), magnetic anisotropy, and magnetic susceptibility (ZFC/FC).

**Important parameters / regimes.**

Fluorine content (approximately F0.1), oxygen excess (delta), and temperature ranges for fluorination (250C to 400C).

**Assumptions / limitations.**

The fluorination process is assumed to be a diffusion-limited mechanism, and the observed magnetic transitions are analyzed in the context of potential NiF2 impurities.

**Figures summary.**

Fig 1 shows crystal structures, Laue patterns, and TGA data; Fig 2 shows XRD Rietveld refinements; Fig 3 displays SEM/EDX images and fluorine elemental maps; Fig 4 presents temperature-dependent magnetization curves.

**Paper structure.**

The paper introduces the problem of bulk fluorination, describes the crystal growth and fluorination methods, presents structural characterization (XRD) and chemical analysis (EDX/TGA), followed by magnetic property measurements and a discussion of the findings.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Topochemical fluorination offers a low--temperature route for modifying the anion chemistry and electronic ground states of layered transition-metal oxides, providing access to metastable phases and functionalities that are not able to be achieved through conventional solid--state synthesis. Despite extensive work on polycrystalline samples and thin films, topochemical fluorination of bulk single crystals has not been studied, limiting insights into intrinsic structure property relationships. Here, we investigate the topochemical fluorination of optical float zone grown (OFZ) La$_2$NiO$_{4+δ}$ single crystals using polymer-based PTFE, PVDF and inorganic CuF$_{2}$ fluorination agents and compare it to our topochemical pathways of reduction of LaNiO$_{3-x}$. By systematically investigating direct and indirect contact reaction pathways, we can understand fluorination mechanisms, quantify the degree of fluorine incorporation, and evaluate the resulting structural and magnetic modifications in a detail that was not possible in powder and thin films. Powder and single--crystal X-ray diffraction reveal that fluorination proceeds without destroying the Ruddlesden--Popper framework, while inducing lattice parameter changes consistent with anion intercalation in the bulk and ion exchange on the surface. This even induces a clear superstructure, which was not reported before and extends the understanding of anion insertion reactions beyond what is known on stage ordering in nickelates. Energy-dispersive X--ray spectroscopy confirms strong fluorine incorporation on the surface and reduced homogeneity in the bulk. Magnetic susceptibility measurements demonstrate a change in antiferromagnetic ordering upon fluorination.

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

<a id="paper-2604.25439"></a>

### [Training cell stress patterns in 3D cellular packings](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.25439v1)

**Authors:** Shabeeb Ameen, Tao Zhang, J. M. Schwarz

**Type:** theory · **Category:** disordered systems and neural networks · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.25439v1>

Constraint load sets an effective capacity, with low values...</sub>

</details>

**Main problem.**

Investigating whether 3D cellular packings can function as trainable matter capable of learning and realizing prescribed internal cellular stress patterns through the evolution of mechanical parameters and network topology.

**Main result.**

The study demonstrates that learning is a collective process where tissue rigidity controls an exploration-exploitation tradeoff, and that increasing constraint load leads to slower, more heterogeneous learning dependent on rare topological rearrangements.

**Method.**

A contrastive learning algorithm is used to update hidden-cell shape indices, combined with a hybrid Langevin-FIRE minimization protocol to handle energy discontinuities during cell rearrangements.

**Summary.**

This paper explores how 3D cellular packings can be trained to achieve specific internal stress patterns, treating the tissue as a nonconventional AI platform. It shows that learning involves both continuous parameter updates and discrete topological rearrangements. The researchers find that the ability to learn depends heavily on the system's rigidity and the density of constraints. Ultimately, the work proposes a framework where biological dysfunction can be viewed as 'mislearning' and suggests that tissues could be engineered as adaptive materials.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.**

A 3D vertex model representing confluent cellular tissue, where cell energy is defined by volume and surface area stiffness, and structural plasticity allows for neighbor exchanges (rearrangements).

**Key observables.**

Cellular stress tensors (maximum shear stress), cell shape indices, topology overlap (rearrangements), and training error/yield.

**Important parameters / regimes.**

Constraint load (ratio of target to total cells), tissue rigidity (controlled by shape index), and training protocol (parallel vs. sequential).

**Assumptions / limitations.**

The model assumes a confluent vertex model with periodic boundary conditions and assumes deformations remain within a linear regime for the clamping subroutine.

**Figures summary.**

Figures show the success of single-cell training, the impact of constraint load on error and parameter distribution, the efficiency difference between sequential and parallel training, and a conceptual phase diagram for learnability.

**Paper structure.**

The paper introduces the concept of learning in reconfigurable tissues, describes the 3D vertex model and contrastive learning algorithm, analyzes the impact of rigidity and constraint load on learning dynamics, compares parallel and sequential training protocols, and concludes with biological implications and a unified theory of learning in collective matter.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

The task of learning patterns is typically associated with systems that update parameters on fixed architectures, such as neural networks, where learning proceeds through continuous optimization. Here, we demonstrate that pattern learning can also emerge in reconfigurable cellular tissue, where both mechanical parameters and network topology evolve. Using a three-dimensional vertex model, we show that cellular packings can be trained to realize prescribed cell stress patterns through a contrastive learning algorithm to update hidden-cell shape indices. We find that learning is intrinsically collective, requiring coordinated, system-wide parameter adjustments, with learnability governed by an interplay between mechanical state, capacity, and training protocol. In particular, the rigidity of the tissue controls an effective exploration-exploitation tradeoff: fluid-like regimes enhance exploration through cellular rearrangements, while rigid regimes constrain dynamics and favor exploitation of existing configurations. These rearrangements introduce discontinuous learning dynamics, enabling the system to transition between distinct local minima in the cost function landscape. As the ratio of target cells to the total number cells in the packing or constraint load increases, learning becomes slower, more heterogeneous, and increasingly dependent on rare rearrangements that allow escape from geometrically constrained states. Finally, training cells in sequence, in contrast to parallel protocols, provides an alternative route that can be more robust but generally takes longer to train for the constraint loads studied. These results suggest a learning phase diagram governed by constraint load, cell packing rigidity, and training protocol. By enabling the training of localized internal states, this work positions tissues not only as adaptive materials, but as nonconventional AI platforms.

</details>

### [Use case study: benchmarking quantum breadth-first search for maximum flow problems](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.24962v1)

**Authors:** Andreea-Iulia Lefterovici, Lara Lelakowski, Michael Perk

**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.24962v1>

**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2604.24962_figures/2604.24962_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>

<sub>Fig. 1: Application of Dinic’s algorithm to find the maximum flow for the graph shown in (a). The BFS finds, as show in (b), three layers, depicted in blue, red and green, respectively. The first path (up) is found by performing a DFS and gives a flow value of f1 = min(10, 17, 17, 10) = 10. The second path (down) yields a flow of f2 = min(15, 30, 17, 15) = 15. At this point, no additional paths can be found, implying that a blocking flow has been reached. The first block of iteration stops. The second block of iteration is depicted in (c) and (d), and takes as an input an updated version of the original the graph, where the remaining capacities have been adjusted. For simplicity, we removed...</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<img src="2604.24962_figures/2604.24962_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>

<sub>Fig. 2: Standard maximum flow benchmark dataset [19], [20] with sizes between 50 and 300,000 vertices.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 3</summary>

<img src="2604.24962_figures/2604.24962_fig3.jpg" width="500"><br>

<sub>Fig. 3: Minimum required time per quantum gate operation to match the execution time of BFS within Dinic’s algorithm for standard classical benchmark datasets. The dashed line at 6.5−9s is the current best achieved time for a gate operation [21]. Since all required times fall below this threshold, achieving a better performance using qBFS is not feasible.</sub>

</details>

**Main problem.**

Evaluating whether the asymptotic speedup of Quantum Breadth-First Search (qBFS) can translate into a practical advantage for solving the maximum flow problem using Dinic's algorithm.

**Main result.**

The study finds that for realistic problem sizes, achieving a quantum advantage would require quantum gate operation speeds that are several orders of magnitude faster than current physical limits.

**Method.**

A hybrid benchmarking approach that uses classical execution of Dinic's algorithm to log BFS runtimes and then analytically estimates the required quantum gate cycles and speeds.

**Summary.**

This paper investigates whether quantum algorithms for graph traversal can provide a practical advantage in solving maximum flow problems. By using a hybrid approach, the authors estimate the hardware requirements needed to outperform classical Dinic's algorithm. They conclude that the required gate speeds for a practical advantage are physically impossible with current or near-term technology. This highlights the gap between asymptotic quantum speedups and practical utility for large-scale network problems.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.**

The study uses flow networks (directed graphs with capacities) and models the quantum component as a QSearch-based implementation of Grover's algorithm to replace classical BFS subroutines.

**Key observables.**

Runtime complexity, number of quantum cycles, and required gate operation times.

**Important parameters / regimes.**

Number of vertices (V), number of edges (E), edge capacities (C), and the physical threshold for gate operation speed (6.5-9 ns).

**Assumptions / limitations.**

Assumes a simplified gate model where one Grover iteration requires 2|L| cycles, assumes the qBFS produces the same layered graph structure as classical BFS, and ignores overheads like error correction or qubit connectivity.

**Figures summary.**

Figure 1 illustrates the execution of Dinic's algorithm on a graph via layered networks; Figure 2 shows the range of problem sizes in the benchmark dataset; Figure 3 compares the required quantum gate speeds to the current physical speed record.

**Paper structure.**

The paper introduces the maximum flow problem and Dinic's algorithm, describes the proposed qBFS substitution, details the hybrid benchmarking methodology and analytical lemmas, presents the results comparing required gate speeds to physical limits, and concludes with a discussion of the implications for quantum advantage.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

The maximum flow problem asks to find the largest possible flow from a source to a sink in a capacitated network. It arises frequently in scheduling, project selection, and as a core subroutine in broader optimisation tasks. Classically, it can be efficiently solved using Dinic's algorithm, which repeatedly performs breadth-first search (BFS) and blocking flow computations on the graph. As a potential candidate for quantum speedups, these BFS subroutines can be naturally replaced with quantum BFS (qBFS), an instantiation of Grover's search algorithm. In this paper, we evaluate the expected performance of qBFS on standard classical datasets. These instances are too large to be solved directly on current quantum hardware, so we adopt a hybrid benchmarking approach: (i) we run a classical implementation of Dinic's algorithm and isolate the runtime of its BFS subroutines; (ii) we analytically estimate the minimum number of quantum cycles required to implement qBFS, where we use the classically logged data. Our results indicate that achieving a practical quantum advantage for realistic problem sizes would translate to quantum gate operation times surpassing physical limitations.

</details>

### [Validity of DFT+U band gaps in all its known functional forms](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.25706v1)

**Authors:** Andrew C. Burgess, David D. O'Regan

**Type:** theory · **Category:** strongly correlated electrons · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.25706v1>

**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2604.25706_figures/2604.25706_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>

The Hubbard U, Hund’s J and intersite V parameters were set at 5 eV, 1 eV and 0 eV, respectively. Seo’s DFT+U functional yields a negative en- ergetic correction for both the spin polarized and non-spin polarized hydrogen lattices of -2 eV and -1 eV, respectively.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 3</summary>

<img src="2604.25706_figures/2604.25706_fig3.jpg" width="500"><br>

<sub>FIG. 3. Correction to the bandgap of the spin polarized hy- drogen lattice at the infinite separation limit upon non-self consistent application of each DFT+U-type functional. The Hubbard U, Hund’s J and intersite V parameters were set at 5 eV, 1 eV and 0 eV, respectively. Two DFT+U functionals, namely Anisimov ’91 and BLOR, reduce the Kohn Sham gap by 5 eV and 2 eV, respectively, while most other functionals increase the Kohn Sham gap of this system.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 4</summary>

<img src="2604.25706_figures/2604.25706_fig4.jpg" width="500"><br>

<sub>FIG. 4. Correction to the bandgap of the non-spin polarized hydrogen lattice at the infinite separation limit upon non-self consistent application of each DFT+U-type functional. The Hubbard U, Hund’s J and intersite V parameters were set at 5 eV, 1 eV and 0 eV, respectively. Most DFT+U functionals fail to modify the Kohn Sham gap of this system.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 5</summary>

<img src="2604.25706_figures/2604.25706_fig5.jpg" width="500"><br>

<sub>FIG. 5. Dudarev’s DFT+U correction to the bandgap ∆σ GKS as a function of the spin-resolved subspace occupancy eigenvalues of a d-orbital subspace under octahedral or tetrahedral crystal field splitting. Under octahedral or tetrahedral crystal field splitting, in the absence of further symmetry breaking, there will be two eigenspaces (of dimensions 2 and 3) of the d-orbital spin-resolved subspace occupancy matrix, the eigenvalues of which we label λσ eg and λσ t2g. The existence of only two distinct spin-resolved subspace occupancy eigenvalues enables the DFT+U bandgap correction of Eq. 106 to be readily plotted as a function of λσ eg and λσ t2g once a suitable model for the orbital...</sub>

</details>

**Main problem.**

Investigating whether the single-particle eigenspectrum gap (eigenvalue gap) in DFT+U and hybrid functionals is a theoretically valid proxy for the fundamental band gap.

**Main result.**

The authors prove that for pristine periodic crystals in the thermodynamic limit, the DFT+U eigenvalue gap exactly matches the fundamental gap, a result that holds across various functional forms and projection operators.

**Method.**

A rigorous analytical derivation using the Generalized Kohn-Sham (GKS) framework, applying Janak's theorem and scaling analysis of functional derivatives with respect to electron count.

**Summary.**

This paper provides a formal theoretical proof that the eigenvalue gap in DFT+U and hybrid functional calculations accurately represents the fundamental band gap for periodic solids. By analyzing the scaling of functional derivatives in the thermodynamic limit, the authors show that the derivative discontinuity term vanishes in the bulk. This resolves long-standing doubts about the conceptual validity of using single-particle spectra as a proxy for total-energy-derived gaps. However, they also clarify that this equivalence breaks down for molecules and defective systems.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.**

The study focuses on pristine, infinite periodic crystals (solids) in the thermodynamic limit, including transition-metal and lanthanide-bearing crystals and the hydrogen lattice in the Mott-Hubbard limit.

**Key observables.**

Fundamental band gap (energy difference between electron addition and removal), single-particle eigenspectrum gap (eigenvalue gap), and total energies.

**Important parameters / regimes.**

Hubbard U and Hund's J parameters, effective Hubbard parameter Ueff, and subspace projection operators.

**Assumptions / limitations.**

The validity is strictly limited to pristine periodic systems with converged k-point sampling; it does not apply to isolated molecules or defective solids.

**Figures summary.**

Figure 1 is a logarithmic scale bar chart showing the widespread utility of the DFT+U method by displaying Google Scholar hits for various DFT software packages.

**Paper structure.**

The paper begins by defining the energy functional and GKS Hamiltonian, proceeds to an analytical proof of the eigenvalue gap validity in the bulk limit, extends the proof to various functional forms and projection methods, and concludes by discussing the limitations regarding defective and isolated systems.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

The Density Functional Theory plus Hubbard $U$ (DFT+$U$) technique is one of the most widely used tools by condensed matter physicists and solid state chemists for the simulation of transition-metal and lanthanide bearing crystals, and increasingly of much more diverse chemistries. Although often synonymous with the corrective functionals of Dudarev et al. and Liechtenstein et al., there exists a wide variety of DFT+$U$-type functionals ready to be utilized, and no doubt yet to be developed. Since the earliest days, the gap in the DFT+$U$ single-particle eigenspectrum has been associated with the fundamental band gap, and the method has typically found more success for spectra than for total-energy derived properties. There has been some doubt, however, as to the conceptual validity of this association. Here, extending findings from recent years regarding local and semi-local functionals, we prove that the DFT+$U$ eigenspectrum gap is indeed valid, in the sense that it matches its own fundamental gap calculated using total-energy differences. This is true for pristine periodic systems with converged $k$-point sampling but not, however, for defective ones or isolated systems. We show that bandgap validity for solids holds in the presence of pseudopotentials and PAW potentials, when using hybrid functionals, and in DFT+$U$(+$J$) irrespective of the level of subspace projection onto the band-edge states. We survey every DFT+$U$-type functional known to have been published to date, within a unified notation. We verify analytically under which conditions the eigenvalue gap equals its fundamental gap for each functional, and analyze its effect on total energies and gaps for the hydrogen lattice in the Mott-Hubbard limit.

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

</details>

### [Temporal hopping dynamics in exciton-polariton condensation](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.25519v1)

**Authors:** Elena Rozas, Wojciech Bukalski, Yannik Brune, Adbhut Gupta, Kirk Baldwin, Loren N. Pfeiffer, Hassan Alnatah, Jonathan Beaumariage, David W. Snoke, Paolo Comaron, Marzena H. Szymanska, Marc Aßmann

**Type:** both · **Category:** statistical mechanics · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.25519v1>

**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

**Topic relevance:** 🔥 `driven-dissipative phase transition` **4/5** · 🔥 `non-equilibrium dynamics` **4/5** · 🔥 `quantum optics experiment` **4/5** · `Keldysh / 2PI / non-Gaussian methods` **3/5** · `methods for driven-dissipative` **3/5** · `non-equilibrium universality` **3/5**

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2604.25519_figures/2604.25519_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>

<sub>Fig. 1: Threshold behavior and instability of the polariton condensate. (a) Measured polariton occupancy (black) and its corresponding PL linewidth (purple) as a function of the normalized pump power. The solid lines serve as guides to the eye. Empty diamonds highlight the pump powers at which detailed measurements are performed: 0.95Pth,1.00Pth,1.06Pth,1.10Pth,1.22Pth and 1.35Pth. Two insets show the real-space image of the uncondensed polaritons (left) and the trapped polariton condensate (right). (b) Average image difference Id, as defined in Eq. (1), around the threshold area. The gray shaded area indicates the range of powers where instabilities of the polariton condensate are...</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<img src="2604.25519_figures/2604.25519_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>

<sub>Fig. 2: Time-resolved photon number Experimental (a-f) and numerically simulated (g-l) photon numbers with increasing pump power. Below threshold (a), no measurable signal is detected due to the strong blueshift of the emission, which prevents the spectral overlap with the local oscillator. Panels (b) and (c) are magnified by a factor of three for clarity.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 3</summary>

<img src="2604.25519_figures/2604.25519_fig3.jpg" width="500"><br>

<sub>Fig. 3: Time-resolved second-order correlation function g(2)(0). Experimental (a-f) and numerical (g-l) g(2)(0) with increasing pump power. Due to the mismatch between signal and LO at 0.95 Pth (a), no signal is detected and therefore, g(2)(0) results in random values.</sub>

</details>

**Main problem.**

Investigating the temporal dynamics and stability of exciton-polariton condensation near the threshold, specifically whether the observed instabilities are intrinsic to the driven-dissipative nature of the system.

**Main result.**

The study discovers a dynamical regime of stochastic 'hopping' between condensed and non-condensed states near the threshold, demonstrating that coherence (reduction of g2(0) toward 1) can emerge even during these intense temporal fluctuations.

**Method.**

Experimental time-resolved homodyne detection and optical trapping are combined with numerical simulations using the stochastic Truncated Wigner Approximation (TWA).

**Summary.**

This paper explores the transition to polariton condensation, revealing that the process is not a static phase transition but involves a period of stochastic hopping between states. By using homodyne detection and TWA simulations, the authors show that the system fluctuates wildly near the threshold while simultaneously building up phase coherence. This demonstrates that the observed instabilities are an intrinsic feature of the driven-dissipative nature of polaritons. The work challenges the idea that a stable, stationary state is required for the emergence of coherence.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.**

Exciton-polariton condensates in a high-Q GaAs microcavity, modeled via a stochastic partial differential equation framework that couples the polariton field to an excitonic reservoir.

**Key observables.**

Second-order correlation function g2(0), photon number, linewidth, and the average image difference (Id) for detecting temporal fluctuations.

**Important parameters / regimes.**

Pump power relative to threshold (P/Pth), specifically identifying an instability window near 1.0 Pth, and the presence of a reservoir-driven driven-dissipative regime.

**Assumptions / limitations.**

The Truncated Wigner Approximation is used, which requires commutator corrections at low densities to remain physically accurate; simulations use a larger pumping ring than the experiment for computational stability.

**Figures summary.**

Fig 1 shows occupancy, linewidth, and image difference vs pump power; Fig 2 compares experimental and numerical time-resolved photon numbers; Fig 3 compares experimental and numerical evolution of g2(0).

**Paper structure.**

The paper introduces the problem of threshold dynamics, presents experimental observations of hopping and coherence buildup, validates these with TWA simulations, and concludes with the implications for driven-dissipative systems.

**Why it may be interesting.**

It provides a deep look into non-equilibrium phase transitions and how quantum coherence can be established in a system characterized by strong fluctuations and stochastic switching, which is central to open quantum systems research.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Polariton condensates provide a versatile platform for exploring non-equilibrium phase transitions and collective phenomena in open quantum systems. Near the condensation threshold, these systems are particularly sensitive to fluctuations and instabilities, which can strongly influence the condensate formation. Using optical trapping and homodyne detection, we directly access the photon statistics and second-order correlation function $g^{(2)}(0)$ of the condensate. We show that polariton condensation near the threshold is not a purely static transition, but instead undergoes a dynamical regime characterized by stochastic hopping between condensed and non-condensed states. These intermittent dynamics are accompanied by a gradual reduction of $g^{(2)}(0)$ towards unity, revealing the progressive build-up of coherence even in the presence of strong temporal fluctuations. Numerical simulations, based on a stochastic Truncated Wigner description of the driven-dissipative polariton field, reproduce these dynamics and capture the essential role of noise and reservoir interactions. This work demonstrates that the observed temporal hopping is an intrinsic feature of polariton condensation, providing a dynamical perspective that goes beyond static descriptions of the condensation phase transition.

</details>

<sub>FIG. 4. Gate-based charge dispersive readout and strong spin-photon coupling in a 3D-integrated hybrid quantum dot cQED device. (a) Transmission amplitude through the feedline as a function of probe frequency at zero magnetic field before forming QDs (e.g. in the Coulomb blockade region). In the single photon limit, we extract an internal quality factor Qi = 10000 and a coupling quality factor Qc = 1600. (b) Double quantum dot stability diagram in the many hole regime dispersively sensed with the microwave cavity. (c) Transmitted amplitude at 5.325 GHz as a function of the voltages applied to the gates G3 and G1G4 at B=0 (note that gate G1 and G4 are shorted together at the device level)....</sub>

</details>

**Main problem.**

Achieving low microwave losses in hybrid circuit QED is difficult because the complex material stacks required for various quantum degrees of freedom often induce high losses. The goal is to develop a 3D-integration process to separate the microwave circuit from the semiconductor device to optimize both independently.

**Main result.**

The authors demonstrated a high-yield 3D-integrated flip-chip architecture using indium bumps that achieves record-sensitivity gate-based dispersive charge readout and strong spin-photon coupling (gs/2pi = 75 MHz).

**Method.**

A 3D flip-chip assembly process was used to bond a silicon MOS spin qubit chip to a NbN superconducting resonator chip using dense indium bumps. The device performance was evaluated through DC daisy-chain testing and RF characterization of various resonator types.

**Summary.**

This paper presents a successful 3D-integration technique for hybrid quantum dot circuit QED using a flip-chip architecture with indium bump interconnects. By separating the silicon MOS qubit chip from the superconducting NbN resonator chip, the researchers overcame the high microwave losses typically associated with complex semiconductor material stacks. The device demonstrates extremely fast and sensitive charge readout and achieves strong coherent coupling between hole spins and microwave photons. This advancement provides a scalable pathway for high-fidelity spin readout and long-range entanglement in quantum computing architectures.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.**

A hybrid circuit QED platform consisting of a semi-industrial silicon MOS hole double quantum dot (DQD) on one chip and a high-impedance (1.7-1.8 kOhm) Niobium Nitride (NbN) superconducting resonator on a sapphire chip, connected via 10 um pitch indium bumps.

**Key observables.**

Internal quality factors (Qi), signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) for readout, charge-photon coupling strength (gc/2pi), spin-photon coupling strength (gs/2pi), and dispersive frequency shifts.

**Important parameters / regimes.**

Indium bump pitch of 10 um, NbN resonator impedance of 1.7-1.8 kOhm, spin-photon coupling gs/2pi = 75 MHz, and charge-photon coupling gc/2pi = 350 MHz.

**Figures summary.**

The figures likely illustrate the 3D integration architecture, the indium bump interconnect properties (resistance and yield), the RF resonator quality factors, the dispersive readout signal strength (SNR), and the avoided crossing in the transmission spectrum demonstrating spin-photon coupling.

**Paper structure.**

The paper introduces the challenge of losses in hybrid cQED, describes the 3D-integration fabrication and assembly process, presents DC and RF characterization of the interconnects, demonstrates the dispersive charge readout performance, and concludes with the demonstration of strong spin-photon coupling.

**Why it may be interesting.**

This work is highly relevant to quantum optics and open quantum systems as it demonstrates a scalable architecture for achieving the strong coupling regime between a solid-state spin qubit and a microwave cavity, which is a fundamental requirement for quantum networks and spin-photon interfaces.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Hybrid circuit quantum electrodynamics (cQED) aims at coupling various quantum degrees of freedom, among which are spin and charge degrees of freedom in gate defined quantum dots, phonons or magnons... with quantized electromagnetic fields in superconducting microwave cavities to investigate fundamental physics questions or for quantum computation and simulation. However, low microwave losses, key for many hybrid cQED experiments, are challenging to achieve given the often exotic and/or complex material stacks (e.g. semiconducting material, ferromagnets, or piezoelectric materials) required to host the various quantum degrees of freedom. In this work, we present a 3D-integration process to overcome this challenge for semi-industrial silicon MOS spin qubits. The process is based on dense indium bump interconnects at a pitch of 10 μm and superconducting thin films of Niobium Nitride (NbN). First, we report on DC and RF interconnect properties that demonstrate a high galvanic interconnection yield and internal quality factors above 105 in the single photon regime for NbN resonators interrupted by a single indium bump interconnect. Eventually, we fabricated a 3D-integrated hybrid circuit quantum electrodynamics (cQED) device based on a semi-industrial MOS hole double quantum dot and a high impedance NbN resonator. For this device, we report a cavity internal quality factor above 10000 and demonstrate record sensitivity for gate-based dispersive readout of the charge degree of freedom with an SNR of 100 in 300 ns. Finally, we demonstrate strong spin-photon coupling of gs/{2π} = 75 MHz, which highlights the viability of 3D-integration for quantum dot based hybrid spin circuit quantum electrodynamics and opens to high-fidelity spin readout and microwave photon-based remote spin qubit entanglement.

</details>

**Main problem.**

The study aims to probe and understand the intrinsic spin dynamics and magnetic noise in CrCl3 van der Waals nanoflakes across its antiferromagnetic, ferromagnetic, and paramagnetic phase transitions.

**Main result.**

The researchers observed a significant enhancement of magnetic noise and a collapse of NV spin coherence in the ferromagnetic phase, identifying intensified GHz-range spin fluctuations and validating these findings with FMR spectroscopy.

**Method.**

The study uses cryogenic diamond quantum sensing microscopy (NV center-based magnetometry) to measure ODMR, Rabi oscillations, and T1 relaxation, complemented by broadband FMR spectroscopy.

**Summary.**

This paper investigates the magnetic phase transitions and spin dynamics of CrCl3 nanoflakes using diamond NV-center microscopy. By measuring spin relaxation and coherence, the authors identify a massive increase in magnetic noise during the ferromagnetic phase. This noise is linked to GHz-frequency spin fluctuations and magnons. The results are significant for developing future 2D magnonic and hybrid quantum-magnon technologies.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.**

The physical system consists of CrCl3 van der Waals nanoflakes (thickness 42-65 nm) interfaced with shallow nitrogen-vacancy (NV) centers in diamond used as nanoscale quantum sensors.

**Key observables.**

NV spin resonance contrast, Rabi oscillation amplitude and decay (Tr), spin-lattice relaxation rate (G1 = 1/T1), and FMR linewidth and resonance field.

**Important parameters / regimes.**

Temperature range of 4-45 K, magnetic fields from 1.45 mT to 40.38 mT, and frequency range of 4-15 GHz.

**Assumptions / limitations.**

The use of a phenomenological additive model assumes that the total relaxation rate can be decomposed into independent contributions from AFM, FM, and PM fluctuation channels.

**Figures summary.**

Figure 1 shows the crystal structure and the cryogenic microscope setup; Figure 2 displays temperature-dependent ODMR spectra; Figure 4 presents the fitted T1 relaxation rate data.

**Paper structure.**

The paper introduces the material and problem, describes the NV microscopy and FMR experimental setup, presents the phase identification via ODMR, details the spin dynamics and noise measurements in different magnetic phases, provides a phenomenological model for the relaxation rate, and concludes with implications for 2D magnonics.

**Why it may be interesting.**

The study provides experimental insights into how many-body spin fluctuations and magnon-mediated noise drive decoherence in a quantum sensor, which is highly relevant to the study of open quantum systems and dissipation in magnetic architectures.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

CrCl3, a layered van der Waals (vdW) magnet, exhibits in-plane magnetic anisotropy and enhanced interlayer coupling upon stacking, making it an ideal platform to host exotic nanoscale magnetic phenomena such as magnon hydrodynamics and meron-like topological spin defects. When interfaced with other vdW materials, its antiferromagnetic-to-ferromagnetic and ferromagnetic-to-paramagnetic phase transitions and magnetic anisotropy can be tuned by voltage, strain, and layer stacking. Understanding the spin dynamics of CrCl3 at its magnetic phase transitions is crucial to its applications in magnonics. Here, we investigate the spin dynamics of CrCl3 nanoflakes using cryogenic diamond quantum sensing microscopy, based on measuring optically detected magnetic resonance, Rabi oscillations, and spin-lattice relaxation time (T1) of shallow nitrogen vacancy (NV) centers in diamond. In the ferromagnetic regime, we observe a pronounced reduction in the NV spin resonance contrast, a collapse of the Rabi oscillations, and a strong enhancement by two orders of magnitude of the relaxation rate, G1 = 1/T1. These observations indicate intensified spin fluctuations in the gigahertz range. Broadband ferromagnetic resonance spectroscopy on CrCl3 microcrystals reveals resonance frequencies in the 4-15 GHz range together with a linewidth of ~24 mT, further supporting the NV measurements. A phenomenological model of magnetic-noise-induced NV relaxation reproduces the temperature dependence of G1 by combining antiferromagnetic, ferromagnetic, and paramagnetic fluctuation channels, indicating that magnetic noise is strongest in the ferromagnetic regime and evolves markedly across the phase diagram. These results are crucial for using CrCl3 in 2D magnonics and hybrid quantum-magnon systems.

</details>

**Authors:** Ritesh Das, Alvaro Bermejillo-Seco, Herre S. J. van der Zant, Peter G. Steeneken, Yaroslav M. Blanter

**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.25588v1>

**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

**Topic relevance:** 🔥 `spintronics-quantum-optics interface` **4/5**

The mechanical displacements are exag- gerated for clarity.</sub>

The external field cants the spins towards the plane. The dashed line represents the saturation field at which the spins lie completely in-plane [30, 41].</sub>

</details>

</details>

</details>

<sub>FIG. 5: (a) Mechanical deformation h and mechanical eigenfrequency as a function of applied gate voltage, ob- tained from COMSOL simulations. The inset shows eigenfrequency as a function of voltage from 0 to 4 V (b) Sin- gle magnon-phonon coupling strength as a function of the mechanical deformation h. The red line shows the ana- lytical result, which neglects the reduction of the mechanical eigenfrequency induced by electrostatic gating. The blue symbols correspond to COMSOL simulations.</sub>

</details>

**Main problem.**

The weak magnon-phonon coupling in conventional bulk platforms like YIG spheres limits the realization of efficient, coherent magnomechanical systems for hybrid information processing.

**Main result.**

Suspended 2D van der Waals ferromagnetic membranes, such as CrGeTe3, exhibit magnomechanical coupling rates up to three orders of magnitude higher than YIG spheres and are highly tunable via electrostatic gating.

**Method.**

The authors use a theoretical framework involving the Holstein-Primakoff transformation and linearized magnetoelastic interactions, supplemented by COMSOL Multiphysics simulations to model electrostatic-induced deformation.

**Summary.**

This paper proposes using suspended 2D van der Waals ferromagnets as a new platform for magnomechanics. By utilizing the large magnetoelastic coupling and the ability to induce pre-strain via electrostatic gating, the authors demonstrate coupling rates significantly higher than those found in traditional YIG-based systems. The study shows that the coupling strength can be tuned by orders of magnitude through voltage-induced deformation. This makes these membranes a robust candidate for developing chip-scale hybrid quantum information processing devices.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.**

A suspended, doubly-clamped 2D van der Waals ferromagnetic membrane (specifically CrGeTe3) with in-plane magnetization, subject to out-of-plane mechanical oscillations and electrostatic pre-strain.

**Key observables.**

Single magnon-phonon coupling rate (g0), mechanical eigenfrequency, cooperativity (C), and static deformation (h).

**Important parameters / regimes.**

Magnon frequencies in the GHz scale, mechanical frequencies in the 10-100 MHz range, coupling rates in the Hz to kHz range, and pre-strain scaling linearly with coupling.

**Assumptions / limitations.**

The model assumes the thin membrane limit (Lz << Ly < Lx), focuses on the Kittel mode, neglects higher-order strain components, and assumes small displacement amplitudes.

**Figures summary.**

Figure 1 shows the 3D and 2D geometry of the membrane; Figure 2 illustrates ferromagnetic resonance and spin alignment; Figure 4 shows coupling vs. pre-strain; Figure 5 compares COMSOL simulations to analytical models for frequency and coupling strength.

**Paper structure.**

The paper introduces the problem of weak coupling in bulk systems, proposes the 2D vdW membrane platform, derives the analytical coupling mechanism and scaling laws, validates the model with numerical simulations of electrostatic tuning, and concludes with the potential for hybrid quantum technologies.

**Why it may be interesting.**

The platform offers a highly tunable, high-cooperativity interface between magnons and phonons, which is a key building block for hybrid quantum transducers and quantum memory in quantum networks.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Magnomechanical systems provide a promising route for exploring coherent hybrid magnon-phonon interactions and hybrid information processing, but their realization has so far been limited by weak magnon-phonon coupling in conventional bulk platforms. We show that a suspended membrane of a two-dimensional van der Waals ferromagnet with in-plane magnetization and out-of-plane mechanical oscillations exhibits large magnomechanical coupling dominated by magnetoelastic interactions. The parametric single magnon-phonon coupling rate scales linearly with pre-strain and can reach hundreds of Hertz to low kiloHertz in suspended membranes of van der Waals ferromagnets such as CrGeTe_3 under experimentally realistic conditions. This rate exceeds typical values reported for YIG spheres by more than three orders of magnitude. Our results demonstrate that suspended membranes of van der Waals magnets provide a robust and highly tunable platform for magnomechanics.

</details>

**Main problem.**

Controlling the nuclear spin population relaxation time (T1) in macroscopic spin ensembles at cryogenic temperatures, where phonon freeze-out leads to prohibitively long relaxation times.

**Main result.**

Demonstrated that 405 nm laser illumination can optically control the 207Pb nuclear T1 relaxation time by creating transient paramagnetic centers (Pb3+ and Ti3+) in ferroelectric crystals.

**Method.**

Used X-band Electron Paramagnetic Resonance (EPR) spectroscopy and saturation-recovery Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (NMR) at 10 K under 405 nm laser illumination.

**Summary.**

This paper demonstrates a method to optically control the nuclear spin relaxation time in lead-based ferroelectric crystals. By using a 405 nm laser, the researchers induce paramagnetic centers that act as a spin bath, effectively shortening the long T1 times caused by cryogenic temperatures. This technique allows for faster thermal polarization and dynamic nuclear polarization. Such control is a critical step toward improving the sensitivity of NMR-based searches for dark matter, such as axion-like particles.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.**

The system consists of 207Pb nuclear spin ensembles in lead-containing ferroelectric crystals (PbTiO3 and PMN-PT). The model relates the nuclear relaxation rate to the density of photoinduced paramagnetic centers using a Zeeman and hyperfine interaction Hamiltonian.

**Key observables.**

EPR spectra (voltage signal), g-factors, linewidths, spin number densities, and nuclear T1 relaxation times.

**Important parameters / regimes.**

Temperature of 10 K; 405 nm laser wavelength; spin densities of approximately 10^17 cm^-3; reduction of T1 from 1550 s to 850 s at 40 MHz.

**Assumptions / limitations.**

Isotropic hyperfine interaction assumption for certain centers; powder-like orientational averaging for Ti3+ due to lattice disorder; treating unresolved hyperfine structure as Gaussian broadening.

**Figures summary.**

Figure 1 shows the experimental setup; Figure 2 shows the EPR spectrum of PT; Figure 7 shows the spatial dependence of the microwave field along the cavity axis.

**Paper structure.**

The paper introduces the problem of long T1 times, describes the experimental setup and materials, details the EPR characterization of paramagnetic centers, presents NMR results for T1 control, and concludes with a model relating relaxation to spin density.

**Why it may be interesting.**

The ability to optically manipulate relaxation rates in a solid-state spin bath is highly relevant to open quantum systems and the development of efficient initialization protocols for quantum sensing and precision metrology.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Macroscopic spin ensembles in solids are powerful platforms for quantum sensing and precision metrology. A key challenge is controlling the nuclear spin population relaxation time $T_1$, which can become prohibitively long at cryogenic temperatures due to phonon freeze-out. We demonstrate optical control of the $T_1$ relaxation time of the $^{207}$Pb nuclear spin ensemble in lead-containing ferroelectric crystals PbTiO$_3$ (PT) and (PbMg$_{1/3}$Nb$_{2/3}$O$_3$)$_{2/3}$-(PbTiO$_3$)$_{1/3}$ (PMN-PT). Using X-band electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectroscopy at 10 K, we characterize light-induced paramagnetic centers created by 405 nm laser illumination. In PT, we observe paramagnetic Pb$^{3+}$ centers and their hyperfine interaction with nearby nuclear spins. In PMN-PT, we identify two populations: isotropic Pb$^{3+}$ centers and anisotropic Ti$^{3+}$ centers occupying $d$-orbitals, with spin number densities of $(2.5 \pm 1.0) \times 10^{17}$ cm$^{-3}$ and $(4.1 \pm 1.7) \times 10^{17}$ cm$^{-3}$, respectively. Power-dependent EPR measurements enable extraction of spin relaxation times. We investigate the ionization and recombination dynamics of these transient paramagnetic centers. Using saturation-recovery nuclear magnetic resonance, we demonstrate that laser illumination reduces the $^{207}$Pb nuclear $T_1$ by approximately a factor of two, from $(17 \pm 2)$ s to $(7 \pm 1)$ s at 4.6 MHz, and from $(1550 \pm 40)$ s to $(850 \pm 70)$ s at 40 MHz. We develop a model relating the nuclear relaxation rate to the density of photoinduced paramagnetic centers. This optical control of nuclear spin relaxation provides a pathway toward accelerated thermal polarization and dynamic nuclear polarization in solid-state NMR-based precision measurements, including searches for axion-like dark matter.

</details>

<a id="paper-2604.25431"></a>

### [From Ultrafast Demagnetization to Ultrafast Spintronics : a 30 years story](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.25431v1)

**Authors:** Quentin Remy, Stéphane Mangin

**Type:** both · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.25431v1>

**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

**Topic relevance:** `non-equilibrium dynamics` **3/5** · `spintronics-quantum-optics interface` **2/5**

<sub>Figure 1. Longitudinal MOKE signal of a Ni(22 nm)/MgF₂(100 nm) film as a function of pump-probe delay20. The pump excitation is a 7 mJ/cm2 pulse with a duration of 60 fs duration. The signal is normalized to the static signal without pump. The sharp drop within a few hundred femtoseconds provides the first direct evidence of ultrafast demagnetization, while the picosecond recovery reflects electron-spin-lattice equilibration. The solid line is a guide to the eye.</sub>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<img src="2604.25431_figures/2604.25431_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>

Electrons, spins, and phonons are described as coupled subsystems, each characterized by an effective temperature, 𝑇!, 𝑇", and 𝑇#, respectively. Energy is exchanged between these reservoirs through phenomenological coupling/time constants, while each subsystem is assumed to remain internally thermalized at all times. A femtosecond laser pulse only acts on the electronic reservoir.</sub>

</details>

(b) The excitation transforms slow majority-spin d electrons (red) into fast sp electrons, thereby launching a spin current towards the gold or ruthenium cap layer. (c) Illustration of the relationship between the spin current, charge current and the spin Hall angle 𝛾.</sub>
